Transition Portrait: Alexander arrived in the UK midway through primary school and is still receiving EAL support to build his academic vocabulary. Despite the language barrier, his mathematical ability is staggering, and he frequently corrects the teacher's calculations on the board. He is eager to fit in and uses football as a universal language to bond with the other boys.
Home Life & Interests: Alexander lives with his mum and aunt; his father remains in Ukraine. The family has faced significant trauma, but Alexander shows incredible resilience and adaptability. He is obsessed with European football and plays daily in the local park, which has been instrumental in helping him build a strong peer group.
Maths: Attacks math problems with high energy and confidence. Spatial awareness and understanding of 3D geometry are excellent. Treats arithmetic like a race. Often misreads the operational sign (+ instead of x) because he is rushing to finish first. Target: Actively highlight the operational signs (+, -, x, ÷) in every question before beginning the calculation.
English: Highly enthusiastic and confident reader. Happy to read aloud and brings great energy to class discussions. Treats reading like a race. Skims over complex vocabulary and frequently misses the emotional subtext or underlying themes. Target: Slow down reading pace. After reading a chapter, summarize the main character's feelings rather than just their actions.
Creative Arts: Alexander treats every single Drama lesson like an action movie—no matter what the scene is supposed to be, he will find a way to add a slow-motion fight or a football tackle! He rushes his art so he can go outside. His target is to act out a calm, completely silent scene (like eating a meal or reading a book) without any imaginary explosions.
PE: Alexander treats every game of football like he is playing in the Premier League! He is highly enthusiastic but overly aggressive, frequently sliding in for tackles on a hard sports hall floor or shoulder-barging smaller students out of the way to get the ball. He could do better if he is strictly penalized for any physical contact during invasion games, forcing him to learn how to intercept and defend using his positioning rather than brute force.
Sciences: Alexander treats the science lab like an action movie! He rushes through instructions, treats plastic pipettes like water pistols, and tries to turn every physics lesson into a wrestling match with gravity. Because safety is paramount, his target is to complete a written "risk assessment" checklist before he is allowed to touch a single piece of practical equipment.
Humanities: Alexander skims right past the social and political context of History so he can get straight to the weapons and the battles! He rushes through his Geography map work, often putting capital cities in the middle of the ocean because he isn't checking his work. He could do better if he is explicitly tasked with researching the civilian experience of a historical event, forcing him to look past the military action.

Transition Portrait: Freya is a bubbly, friendly, and highly sociable girl who loves being at school primarily for the friendships. Her academic focus is easily derailed by a desire to chat, and she can be led astray by more dominant personalities in her friendship group. She benefits from strict seating plans that place her alongside positive, focused role models.
Home Life & Interests: Freya lives with her mum, and they have a very close, almost sisterly relationship. They spend a lot of time baking together and doing various craft projects, which Freya loves to bring in and share with staff. She has a happy home life but lacks a quiet, dedicated space to complete her homework, often doing it in front of the TV.
Maths: Has an intuitive, natural grasp of basic algebra, missing number problems, and generating linear sequences. Struggles significantly with concentration during routine, repetitive arithmetic practice. Target: Sustain focus and maintain accuracy during 15-minute blocks of independent arithmetic practice.
English: A sensitive and empathetic reader. Creates beautifully inclusive and thoughtful characters in creative writing. High anxiety means they often delete their work or refuse to submit it, fearing it isn't "good enough." Target: Save all drafts in a dedicated folder and review them with a teacher to build confidence in their own unique voice.
Creative Arts: Frey is a very sweet, sensitive student who comes up with lovely, kind characters in drama games. Because they are so anxious, they often hide their artwork under their arms so no one can see it, and they will silently mouth the words during choir practice rather than actually singing. A great target for them is to show their sketchbook just to the teacher once a week to slowly build their confidence.
PE: Frey is our second active refuser. Because of their severe social anxiety, they "forget" their PE kit almost every single week to avoid the sensory overload of the changing rooms and the fear of making a mistake in front of loud, aggressive peers. They could do better in Year 7 if they are allowed to change privately and are gently reintroduced to PE through a supporting role, like scorekeeper or equipment manager, before joining small-group activities.
Sciences: Frey is a very thoughtful student who is great at topics like ecology and the environment. Because they are so anxious, the idea of using glass beakers or Bunsen burners terrifies them, and they are constantly worried about breaking something or doing it "wrong." Their target is to build their lab confidence by working with a highly trusted partner, focusing on completing just one safe, practical step per lesson.
Humanities: Frey is a deeply empathetic student who found our topic on WWII evacuees very emotional, writing a truly beautiful and sensitive diary entry. Because they are highly anxious, the loud, argumentative debates we sometimes have in History or RE cause them to shrink back and cover their ears. They could do better by having a designated "quiet role" during class debates, such as being the official minute-taker who writes down everyone's points safely from the sidelines.

SEND Detail: Suspected MLD. Generalised slow cognitive processing. Needs concepts broken down.
Transition Portrait: Hannah finds almost all academic subjects incredibly difficult due to a generally slow processing speed and poor retention. However, her resilience is remarkable; she never complains, she always tries her absolute best, and she is a delight to teach. She needs heavily differentiated work, visual scaffolds, and constant praise for her incredible work ethic.
Home Life & Interests: Hannah lives with her mum, who is very supportive but acknowledges Hannah's struggles with learning. Outside of school, Hannah is heavily involved in martial arts and is working toward her black belt in karate. The repetition, discipline, and clear visual progression of the belt system suit her learning style perfectly and give her immense pride.
Maths: Shows immense resilience despite her MLD. Making steady progress and is now confident adding and subtracting 4-digit numbers. Retention of times tables and multi-step reasoning is highly challenging. Needs constant visual reinforcement to retain methods. Target: Use concrete manipulatives (like Numicon or Dienes blocks) to secure a conceptual understanding of multiplication before moving to written methods.
English: Shows wonderful resilience. Tries exceptionally hard in reading comprehension and contributes safely during small group reading. Moderate Learning Difficulty (MLD) means decoding complex words and retaining narrative structure over a whole novel is very tiring. Target: Use visual storyboards to map out the plot of class readers, reducing the cognitive load of holding the story in her working memory.
Creative Arts: Hannah is incredibly resilient and loves the messy, sensory side of Art, especially using clay or finger paints! Because of her learning difficulties, trying to memorize a whole song or a long dance routine in Drama is very overwhelming and she gets upset when she forgets the steps. Her target is to focus on learning just one short, repeating chorus or rhythm loop, rather than worrying about the whole performance.
PE: Hannah shows wonderful resilience and loves the social aspect of team games. Because of her learning difficulties, complex, multi-stage rules (like the offside rule or the footwork rules in netball) are very confusing for her, and she frequently gets penalized without understanding why. She could do better if she plays heavily modified, simplified versions of games where the focus is purely on throwing, catching, and moving, rather than strict regulation rules.
Sciences: Hannah loves hands-on, messy science like making slime or planting seeds! Because of her learning difficulties, trying to remember a multi-step method or a long list of safety rules is very overwhelming, and she can easily make a dangerous mistake. Her target is to use physical, picture-based safety cards on her desk to remind her of the steps, rather than relying on a written worksheet.
Humanities: Hannah shows wonderful resilience and loves hands-on Humanities tasks like drawing artifacts. Because of her learning difficulties, trying to remember a long sequence of historical events or complex geographical terms is very overwhelming. She could do better in Year 7 if she uses comic-strip storyboards to map out historical events, reducing the cognitive load of holding the narrative in her head.

Transition Portrait: Harper has a lovely, gentle demeanor and is unfailingly polite to staff and peers alike. However, she lacks academic confidence and will often rub out correct answers because she second-guesses herself. She is terrified of getting things wrong and needs a classroom culture where mistakes are celebrated as part of the learning process.
Home Life & Interests: Harper lives with her mum in a very quiet, calm household. She is a highly creative and meticulous girl who loves crafting, specifically making intricate beaded jewelry, which she sometimes tries to sell to family members. This hobby highlights her excellent fine motor skills and patience, traits she struggles to apply to academic writing.
Maths: Exceptionally meticulous in practical geometry (using protractors/rulers). Presentation is immaculate. Experiences severe maths anxiety which triggers a freeze response. Panic often sets in before she has even read the question, causing her mind to go completely blank. Target: Focus on emotional regulation over calculation; use a "traffic light" card on her desk to silently indicate her anxiety levels to the teacher before starting a task.
English: Produces beautifully presented work with flawless spelling, punctuation, and grammar. High anxiety when asked to share ideas verbally; terrified of getting the "wrong" answer during literature analysis. Target: Write down one point of analysis during pair-share and read it directly from the page to build speaking confidence.
Creative Arts: Harper's artwork is so tiny and meticulous you almost need a magnifying glass to see it! Because of her severe anxiety, the loud, unpredictable nature of Drama lessons terrifies her, and she will completely freeze if asked to improvise. Her target is to use a "traffic light" card on her desk or in her pocket to silently signal to the teacher if she is feeling too overwhelmed to participate.
PE: Harper has excellent stamina and is a very strong cross-country runner where she can just focus on her own breathing. However, the chaotic nature of team sports terrifies her; she is so anxious about making a mistake and letting her team down that she will actively avoid the ball. She could do much better in Year 7 if her teamwork is built up slowly through low-pressure, cooperative games (like keeping a balloon in the air as a group) before she is asked to play competitive netball.
Sciences: Harper's science book is beautifully neat, but her severe anxiety means the actual lab work terrifies her. She is constantly worried about dropping a glass beaker or spilling a chemical and getting into trouble, so she will often refuse to touch the equipment. Her target is to build her confidence by using plastic equipment where possible, and having a specific "safety checker" role to make her feel in control.
Humanities: Harper's History work is beautifully presented, but her severe anxiety means she is terrified of getting a date or a historical fact "wrong." When we do map reading in Geography, she often freezes up entirely, worried she will plot a coordinate incorrectly. She could do much better in Year 7 if she is given a pre-marked map to start with, building her confidence by finding locations before she is asked to plot them herself.

Transition Portrait: Muhammad is a quiet, thoughtful boy who shines in mathematics, where he can manipulate numbers and spot patterns effortlessly. He lacks confidence in English, particularly when analyzing complex texts or poetry, and will often try to hide at the back of the room during reading sessions. He benefits from pre-reading texts before the lesson to build his confidence.
Home Life & Interests: Muhammad lives with his parents and is heavily involved in his local mosque community. His primary passion outside of school is cricket; he is a talented spin bowler and plays for a local club. His father is very supportive of his sporting ambitions but regularly reminds him that his academics must come first.
Maths: A deeply capable, quiet mathematician. Has a superb grasp of ratio, proportion, and elegantly solving scale-factor problems. Extremely reserved. Will rarely, if ever, ask for help when he encounters a concept he doesn't understand. Target: Build confidence by verbally explaining a successfully completed mathematical concept to a peer once a week.
English: Deeply perceptive and thoughtful reader who picks up on subtle character nuances that others miss. Extremely quiet; will rarely volunteer to read aloud or share his excellent insights in whole-class discussions. Target: Prepare one written contribution in advance to share confidently during small-group (rather than whole-class) discussions.
Creative Arts: Muhammad is a very gentle boy who will quietly draw the most amazing, detailed little sketches in the margins of his books. He is painfully shy, though, and if you ask him to share his ideas for a play with the whole class, he will completely freeze up and look at the floor. To help him find his voice, his target is to share his ideas with just one trusted talk-partner rather than the whole room.
PE: Muhammad is incredibly fast and has amazing footwork, often darting through the entire opposing team during tag rugby! Because he is so quiet, he will never call for the ball, meaning his teammates often ignore him, and he will silently accept being left out. He could do better if he is challenged to project his voice, specifically practicing shouting his teammates' names loudly to demand the ball during matches.
Sciences: Muhammad is a very gentle, quiet boy who is incredibly observant. During a chemical reaction, he will notice the tiny color changes or the small bubbles that everyone else misses. Because he is so shy, he will never put his hand up to share these brilliant observations. To help him find his voice, his target is to be the designated "data recorder" in his group, ensuring his sharp observations are captured on paper.
Humanities: Muhammad is a very gentle boy who thinks deeply about the moral questions we raise in RE, like poverty or forgiveness. Because he is practically silent in class, it is very easy to forget he is there, and he never volunteers to read historical sources aloud. He could do better by sharing his insights with just one trusted talk-partner during a "think-pair-share" rather than facing the whole room.

Transition Portrait: Arthur is a highly confident and charismatic boy who loves the sound of his own voice and will happily dominate any class discussion. He is intelligent and well-read, but he needs to learn how to actively listen to his peers rather than just waiting for his turn to speak. He thrives when given leadership roles but must be monitored so he doesn't become bossy.
Home Life & Interests: Arthur comes from an affluent background and lives with his parents near the waterfront. He is actively involved in the local yacht club and spends his summers learning to sail dinghies. This hobby has given him a strong sense of independence and an understanding of weather and physics, which he frequently brings up in science lessons.
Maths: Calculates complex fractions, decimals, and percentages (FDP) conversions mentally with impressive speed. Arrogance leads to careless errors. Frequently refuses to write down his methods because he views it as "beneath" his ability. Target: Must demonstrate full, step-by-step working out to earn the "method mark," regardless of whether the final answer is correct.
English: Highly confident reader and speaker. Writes persuasive texts with an authoritative, commanding tone. Arrogant approach to drafting; frequently refuses to proofread or edit his "perfect" first drafts, leaving careless errors. Target: Actively identify, highlight, and improve at least three specific sentences in every first draft before final submission.
Creative Arts: Arthur is very loud, very confident, and loves being the star of the show! He can be a bit arrogant, though; he usually finishes his art projects in ten minutes, declares them a masterpiece, and completely refuses to add any more detail or listen to feedback. His target is to ask one friend for a "wish" (something to improve) on his drawing, and he must add it before he's allowed to pack his pens away.
PE: Arthur genuinely believes he is the next Lionel Messi. He is very skilled, but he refuses to play any position other than striker, will attempt ridiculous trick-shots instead of scoring simply, and absolutely refuses to pass to girls or peers he deems "bad" at sports. He could do much better if he is strictly assigned to play in defense for a whole term, forcing him to learn tackling, positioning, and how to protect his team.
Sciences: Arthur is very confident and absolutely loves the "bangs and flashes" of science! He is quite arrogant, though, and will often just guess the results of an experiment or rush through it in two minutes without actually reading the method, leading to completely useless data. His target is that he must write down the step-by-step method before he is allowed to touch a single piece of equipment.
Humanities: Arthur is very loud, very confident, and loves dominating debates about whether the Vikings were actually just misunderstood traders! He is incredibly arrogant about his written work, though, frequently handing in history essays that are full of factual errors because he rushed them. He could do better if he explicitly proofreads his work to check three specific dates or facts against a textbook before he is allowed to hand it in.

Transition Portrait: Lucas finds the cognitive load of a secondary curriculum very challenging and struggles to keep up with the pace of mainstream lessons. He is eager to please but will often nod and say he understands when he is actually entirely lost. He relies heavily on the school's after-school homework club to get his assignments done, as he needs 1-to-1 scaffolding.
Home Life & Interests: Lucas lives with his dad, who works long hours and admits he struggles to understand the modern curriculum to help with homework. Lucas is a quiet boy who retreats into the world of Minecraft in his downtime, where he builds elaborate redstone machines. This shows he has great spatial awareness and logic skills when engaged.
Maths: Has strong underlying ability, particularly in mental arithmetic and rapidly calculating percentages of amounts. Extreme fatigue from late-night gaming means his processing speed and stamina during written assessments have dropped significantly. Target: Chunk assessments and extended tasks into highly focused 15-minute intervals to manage cognitive load and fatigue.
English: Strong intuitive understanding of narrative structure, world-building, and character arcs (likely stemming from RPG gaming). Chronic fatigue means his handwriting degrades rapidly into illegibility, and he frequently zones out during silent reading. Target: Break reading tasks into short, active chunks (e.g., read two pages, summarize out loud) to maintain wakefulness and focus.
Creative Arts: Lucas is a massive gamer, which shows because all of his artwork is just copying video game logos! He is chronically exhausted from late nights, so he frequently yawns loudly through choir practice or zones out entirely during script reading. His target for Year 7 is to participate in short, highly active physical theatre games to get him moving and wake him up.
PE: Lucas loves strategy and has great spatial awareness on the pitch, likely from his gaming! Because he is so chronically exhausted, he rarely has the energy for a full forty-minute football match and will often just stand completely still in the midfield, letting players run right past him. He could do better if he is given a high-focus, low-running role like goalkeeper or wicket-keeper, keeping him mentally engaged in the game without draining his limited physical energy.
Sciences: Lucas is chronically exhausted from late-night gaming, meaning he frequently zones out during the teacher's explanation of the method. When it's time to start the practical, he will just sit there staring blankly at the desk. His target is to actively wake up his brain by being the designated person in his group who must stand up, walk to the front, and collect the heavy equipment tray.
Humanities: Lucas loves learning about the tactics of historical battles, drawing heavily on his gaming knowledge! Because he is chronically exhausted, he frequently falls asleep when asked to read long, densely printed historical sources or textbooks. He could do better if he is provided with audio-recorded versions of historical sources or allowed to use interactive, digital quizzes to keep his brain actively engaged.

Transition Portrait: Mia is a highly capable, mature, and independent learner who requires very little teacher direction to excel. She takes great pride in the presentation of her work and often acts as a quiet leader in group projects. Occasionally, she puts too much pressure on herself to be perfect and needs reassurance that it is okay to make mistakes.
Home Life & Interests: Mia is a registered young carer who takes on significant household responsibilities to support her mother, who has a chronic health condition. Despite her responsibilities, she rarely complains and uses school as a safe haven. She loves escaping into fantasy novels and is a voracious reader, often finishing a book every few days.
Maths: Highly methodical approach. Excels in statistics, data handling, and constructing accurate graphs. Occasionally lacks confidence when introduced to abstract concepts, particularly early algebra and missing number problems. Target: Build confidence in substituting numerical values into basic algebraic formulae.
English: Shows deep, mature empathy during reading comprehension. Writing tone is highly sensitive and thoughtful. Frequently misses reading homework and extended writing assignments due to her young carer responsibilities at home. Target: Maximize in-class drafting time and utilize designated library/tutor time for independent reading.
Creative Arts: Mia is a very kind and thoughtful girl, which really shows in her careful drawings and the way she plays characters in Drama. Unfortunately, she is often very tired from helping out at home, so expecting her to do big art projects for homework just isn't fair. Her target for Year 7 is to have all her Art and Music assessments based entirely on what she finishes in the classroom.
PE: Mia is very graceful and excels in expressive dance and gymnastics routines, working wonderfully well in pairs. Because she is a young carer, she frequently comes to school too exhausted for high-stamina sports like long-distance running, and she often forgets her PE kit entirely. She could do better if the school keeps a spare, clean PE kit permanently in her locker, removing the pressure of remembering it from home.
Sciences: Mia is a wonderfully sensible and calm lab partner who is particularly gentle and thoughtful during nature and habitat topics. She is often very tired from her caring responsibilities at home, meaning she struggles to complete long homework tasks like researching a scientist or finishing a graph. Her target is to ensure all her heavy, practical science learning happens in the classroom, keeping her homework strictly to quick, low-stakes quizzes.
Humanities: Mia shows such beautiful, mature empathy in RE; she truly understands the moral lessons behind parables and is deeply thoughtful when learning about different world religions. Sadly, her caring duties at home mean she is frequently too exhausted to complete heavy research homework on the Victorians or Rivers. She could do better if she is assessed on her deep verbal contributions in class, completely removing the pressure of extensive written homework.

Transition Portrait: Penelope is a beacon of positivity in the classroom; she is highly creative, enthusiastic, and brings out the best in her peers during group work. She is a natural performer and loves subjects like Drama and Music. She can sometimes become a bit too "chatty" and needs gentle reminders to lower her volume, but her intentions are always good.
Home Life & Interests: Penelope has a very supportive home life with her parents, who encourage her creative pursuits. She is a talented musician who sings and plays the acoustic guitar, often performing at local open mic nights. She has a strong sense of personal style and loves expressing herself through vintage fashion and bold accessories.
Maths: Enjoys the creative, visual side of math, such as drawing geometric patterns, symmetry, and reflecting shapes. Actively avoids rigorous arithmetic practice. Needs to secure her formal written method for long division. Target: Break down long division into a rhythmic, memorable chant or checklist to help her retain the operational steps.
English: Brings incredible theatricality to her reading. Her creative writing is bursting with artistic, bohemian flair. Written layout is completely chaotic. Paragraphing is non-existent, making her brilliant ideas very difficult to follow. Target: Strictly apply the TiPToP rule (change paragraph for Time, Place, Topic, or Person) to structure all narrative writing.
Creative Arts: Penelope is an absolute diva in the best way possible—she lives for Drama and will bring in her own props and costumes from home! Her art is a massive, colorful explosion of glitter, glue, and feathers, and she completely ignores the cleanup bell. Her target is to stop her practical work five minutes early to actually wash her paintbrushes and tidy her space.
PE: Penelope treats the sports hall like a West End stage! She will frequently stop in the middle of a serious football match to do a perfectly executed cartwheel or strike a pose, completely ignoring the actual game happening around her. She would improve if she is given strict boundaries, keeping her dramatic flair confined to the dance and gymnastics modules while enforcing strict positional discipline during team sports.
Sciences: Penelope treats the science lab like her own personal art studio. She loves mixing colors and liquids, but she is completely chaotic and leaves a massive trail of spilled water, used pipettes, and dirty beakers across her desk. Her target for Year 7 is to stop her practical work five minutes before everyone else to complete a mandatory, supervised clean-up routine.
Humanities: Penelope treats History like a soap opera! When asked to write a factual report on the Tudors, she will write a dramatic, highly fictionalized romance story that contains absolutely zero historical facts. Her written presentation is completely chaotic. She would improve if she is required to highlight at least five historically accurate facts in her writing before it is considered finished.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed ODD & Attachment Trauma. Highly reactive to perceived authority challenges.
Transition Portrait: Jack finds the classroom environment incredibly overwhelming and struggles significantly with emotional regulation, often entering a 'fight or flight' state. If given direct, uncompromising commands, he will immediately push back and escalate the situation. He needs highly predictable routines, visual timetables, and a designated safe space to retreat to when he feels his anxiety rising.
Home Life & Interests: Jack's home life is complex; he is currently under a Special Guardianship Order and social care is actively involved. He finds transitions between home and school very difficult, often arriving highly dysregulated. On his good days, he is fascinated by mechanics and spends hours with his uncle taking apart small engines and learning how to rebuild them.
Maths: Demonstrates a very solid grasp of number fluency and basic operations when he feels emotionally secure in the classroom. Frequently refuses to attempt complex or multi-step reasoning tasks, becoming easily overwhelmed and oppositional. Target: Build academic resilience by breaking down multi-step reasoning problems into single, manageable calculations.
English: Can write incredibly powerful, punchy, and emotive sentences when he feels safe and engaged with the topic. ODD (Oppositional Defiant Disorder) triggers complete task refusal during extended, silent writing blocks. Target: Break writing assessments into micro-tasks or "contracts" (e.g., "Write three sentences, then you may take a two-minute break").
Creative Arts: When Jack is sitting quietly by himself, he draws some really cool, intense sketches. Because he is so anxious and gets defensive easily, he flatly refuses to stand up and perform in front of the class, and pushing him to do it will cause a meltdown. His target for Year 7 is to be allowed to film his drama or music tasks privately on a tablet with just one friend, rather than performing for the room.
PE: Jack is a complete refuser when it comes to structured PE. He hates the echoey sports hall, refuses to wear the school PE uniform, and will literally sit on the bench with his back to the class rather than join in a basketball drill. He actually loves climbing trees and riding his bike outside of school. He could do much better if the uniform rules are relaxed for him and he is engaged through alternative, outdoor adventurous activities like orienteering or mountain biking.
Sciences: Jack actually knows a huge amount about nature, bugs, and wildlife from spending time alone outside. Because of his ODD, if a teacher demands he put on safety goggles or follow a strict rule, he will immediately refuse just to challenge the authority. A great target for him is to be given the official job of "table safety inspector," giving him a sense of ownership over the rules rather than feeling like he is just being bossed around.
Humanities: Jack spends a lot of time outdoors and actually knows more about local habitats, weather, and nature than most of the adults! Because of his ODD, if a teacher demands he sit down and fill out a worksheet on the rainforest, he will push it onto the floor and refuse. He could do better if he is given independent, self-directed research projects on environmental geography, giving him autonomy over how he presents his knowledge.

SEND Detail: Suspected Working Memory Deficit. Forgets multi-step instructions instantly.
Transition Portrait: Joseph is incredibly eager to please but suffers from a severely limited working memory. If a teacher gives three instructions (e.g., "Write the date, underline it, and open to page 4"), he will only remember the last one. He requires instructions to be given one at a time, backed up with visual cues on the board, and frequent check-ins to ensure he is on task.
Home Life & Interests: Joseph lives with his mum and stepdad and is a very practical, outdoorsy boy. His absolute favorite thing to do is go sea fishing with his stepdad at the weekends. He can tie complex knots and identify dozens of fish species, showing that his memory works perfectly when the information is tied to a physical action and a personal interest.
Maths: Has a great practical grasp of weight, capacity, and measurement. Highly enthusiastic and eager to please. Working memory is weak. Frequently forgets basic times tables, requiring constant, low-stakes repetition to build fluency. Target: Keep a physical multiplication grid on his desk during all reasoning tasks to reduce cognitive load.
English: Loves outdoor adventure narratives. Highly enthusiastic and always eager to contribute his ideas verbally. Weak working memory means he frequently forgets spelling rules (like "i before e") and loses the thread of longer narratives. Target: Create a visual "story map" on his desk to track key plot points and character arcs while reading class novels.
Creative Arts: Joseph is so enthusiastic and will happily bang on the drums or jump around in Drama! But his poor working memory means he loses his paintbrush every five minutes, forgets the dance routine instantly, and can never remember his cues. His target is to be the "mirror" in Drama, just copying exactly what his partner does so he doesn't have to remember a sequence.
PE: Joseph is wonderfully enthusiastic and will happily run all day! His dyscalculia and weak working memory mean he frequently forgets the score, forgets which direction his team is supposed to be shooting, and forgets the rules of complex games like rounders. He could do better if he is paired with a reliable "sports buddy" who handles the scoring and positional reminders, allowing Joseph to just focus on the physical joy of running and catching.
Sciences: Joseph is highly enthusiastic, but his dyscalculia and weak working memory make counting very difficult. If an experiment requires "five drops of iodine," he will lose count at three and just squirt the rest in, ruining the test. His target is to keep a physical tally chart on his desk, marking off each single drop as he adds it to reduce the cognitive load.
Humanities: Joseph is highly enthusiastic, but his dyscalculia and weak working memory make History very difficult; he frequently mixes up centuries and cannot remember four-figure grid references in Geography. He could do better if he has a permanent, visual timeline taped to his desk in History, and a step-by-step visual guide for reading coordinates in Geography.

Transition Portrait: Lily is the bedrock of any classroom—sensible, grounded, and consistently making good choices. She is not the loudest or the highest achiever, but she is the student teachers rely on to pair with new or struggling peers because of her unending patience. She occasionally needs reminding to focus on her own extension tasks rather than just helping others.
Home Life & Interests: Lily has a very warm, traditional home life with her parents and is closely connected to her extended family in the area. She is highly athletic and plays center for a local junior netball team, bringing her teamwork and communication skills from the court directly into the classroom.
Maths: An incredibly reliable mathematician who consistently meets all KS2 targets. Lays out working systematically and clearly. Can occasionally lack the confidence to assert her correct methods when placed in a dominant or loud group-work setting. Target: Act as a lead peer-mentor to gently guide struggling classmates through formal KS3 calculation methods.
English: A highly reliable student who consistently meets all targets; reads widely and writes coherent, well-structured essays. Tends to blend into the background and plays it safe with her vocabulary rather than taking creative or intellectual risks. Target: Actively use a thesaurus to find, define, and incorporate three "ambitious" new words into every piece of extended writing.
Creative Arts: Lily is a lovely, reliable girl who will happily paint the background scenery blue while the louder kids take all the fun jobs. She blends completely into the background and never puts herself forward for a solo or a main acting part. Her target to boost her confidence is to be put in a "pair" rather than a big group for Drama, forcing her to take on 50% of the acting work.
PE: Lily is wonderfully reliable; she always has the correct kit, listens to instructions perfectly, and jogs sensibly around the track. She completely blends into the background during invasion games, however, hovering on the edge of the pitch and never actively tackling or calling for a pass. She would improve if she is made the team captain for a very small-sided game (like 3v3 basketball), forcing her to take a central, active role where she cannot hide.
Sciences: Lily is a lovely, reliable girl, but she completely blends into the background during group work. She will happily take on the role of "cleaner," washing out all the beakers and wiping the desk while her louder lab partner actually gets to do the fun melting, mixing, and measuring. Her target is to take the physical lead on the experiment at least once a week while her partner is forced to do the cleaning.
Humanities: Lily is a lovely, reliable girl who always listens carefully during Religious Education and completes her History worksheets perfectly. She is so quiet, though, that she completely blends into the background during class debates on moral issues, never offering her own opinion. She could do better in Year 7 if she is assigned the role of "chairperson" for a small group of three, forcing her to gently lead the conversation.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed DLD. Receptive language age approx. 6 years.
Transition Portrait: Sophia wants desperately to learn and join in, but the language barrier of her DLD means she often misinterprets instructions and becomes lost. She requires heavily differentiated resources, visual cues for every step of a task, and instructions broken down into single sentences. She thrives in practical, hands-on subjects like art where language isn't a barrier.
Home Life & Interests: Sophia currently lives with a long-term foster family who are incredibly dedicated to her progress and use Makaton at home to support her communication. She finds the world a confusing place but finds great comfort in highly tactile, sensory experiences. She loves painting, molding clay, and helping her foster dad in the garden.
Maths: Incredibly hardworking and entirely secure in her core arithmetic. Never gives up on a calculation. DLD (Developmental Language Disorder) makes decoding wordy, multi-step reasoning problems highly challenging. Target: Use a highlighter to isolate key mathematical command words and critical numbers before attempting a word problem.
English: Incredible work ethic; tries exceptionally hard to apply SPaG rules accurately in all her writing. DLD (Developmental Language Disorder) makes decoding figurative language (metaphors/similes) and complex vocabulary very difficult. Target: Create a personalized visual glossary for each new class text to support vocabulary retention and comprehension.
Creative Arts: Sophia works so hard and always tries her best to remember the dance routines or the lyrics to a song. Because of her language disorder, she gets really confused if a drama game involves abstract rules, like "mime a feeling" or "act out a metaphor." Moving into Year 7, her target is to always have a visual picture or a physical prop to hold so she understands exactly what the drama scene is about.
PE: Sophia works incredibly hard and always brings a positive, cheerful attitude to PE, especially during dance! Due to her Developmental Language Disorder, she really struggles to understand the complex rules of new games if they are just explained verbally by the teacher. She could do better if the teacher uses a whiteboard to draw out the pitch and uses a physical, slow-motion demonstration of the game rules before blowing the whistle to start.
Sciences: Sophia works incredibly hard and loves taking part in the practical experiments. Due to her Developmental Language Disorder, she struggles significantly to remember complex scientific vocabulary like "photosynthesis," "evaporation," or "condensation." Moving into Year 7, her target is to create a visual science glossary for every new topic, using pictures to help her remember what the big words actually mean.
Humanities: Sophia works so incredibly hard and loves looking at the historical artifacts we bring into class. Due to her Developmental Language Disorder, abstract Humanities vocabulary like "incarnation," "monarchy," or "erosion" is practically impossible for her to retain from verbal instructions alone. She could do better by building a visual flashcard deck for every new History and Geography topic, matching the big words to simple pictures.

Transition Portrait: Alice is a highly practical, hands-on learner who shines in subjects like Food Tech, Art, and Drama. However, she struggles significantly with homework completion and personal organization, frequently arriving without her books or PE kit. She is easily overwhelmed by large, text-heavy tasks and needs them broken down into manageable bullet points.
Home Life & Interests: Alice lives with her dad and four siblings in a highly chaotic, noisy household. As one of the older siblings, she takes on a lot of domestic chores, including cooking dinner several nights a week. Because her home life is so busy, she has virtually no quiet space or time to complete homework, leading to frequent detentions.
Maths: Maintains a solid grasp of KS2 arithmetic and shows wonderful determination during class time despite her exhaustion. Frequently misses math homework due to her caring responsibilities, which creates gaps in her independent consolidation. Target: Maximize in-class guided practice time to ensure she secures the method before leaving the room, reducing the burden of homework.
English: An incredibly empathetic reader; understands complex family dynamics and emotional subtext in novels intuitively. Rarely completes reading or writing homework due to her caring duties, which limits her exposure to wider vocabulary. Target: Utilize form time, library lessons, or homework club to ensure she has a guaranteed, quiet 20 minutes for independent reading.
Creative Arts: Alice is a deeply empathetic girl who gives lovely, believable performances when she has the energy. Because she helps care for her family, she often comes to school exhausted and forgets to bring things like coloring pencils or her script. Her target is to be given a quiet, safe corner to do some relaxed doodling if she is too tired for a loud Drama lesson, with all her supplies kept safely at school.
PE: Alice is a wonderful team player when she has the energy, always cheering on her peers and playing fairly. Because she is a young carer, she frequently forgets her PE kit or comes to school too exhausted to participate in high-intensity fitness testing. She could do better if the school quietly provides a clean, spare kit for her to use, and allows her to participate as a scorekeeper or line judge on the days she is too tired to run.
Sciences: Alice is a deeply empathetic girl who loves learning about animals, habitats, and human biology. Because she is a young carer, she often comes to school too exhausted to deal with the loud, messy chaos of a chemistry practical, and she frequently forgets her homework. Her target is to ensure all her heavy cognitive learning happens in the lab, keeping her homework strictly to quick, low-stakes online quizzes.
Humanities: Alice has a profound understanding of historical hardship and writes deeply moving, empathetic responses when learning about refugees or WWII evacuees. Because she is a young carer, expecting her to complete large Geography or History research projects at home is completely unrealistic and causes her stress. She could do better if all of her Humanities project work is strictly contained within lesson time or a lunchtime homework club.

Transition Portrait: Oliver is an absolute pleasure to teach; he is an excellent team player who navigates social dynamics with ease. He thrives in practical lessons like science experiments or DT, where he can use his hands to solve problems. While his reading comprehension is solid, he rarely reads for pleasure and needs encouragement to tackle more challenging texts.
Home Life & Interests: Oliver lives with his mum, who works shifts at Derriford Hospital, meaning he often stays at an after-school club. He is a phenomenal athlete with a true passion for track and field, specifically sprinting. He trains multiple times a week with a local athletics club and dreams of competing at a national level.
Maths: Highly confident with position, direction, and measurement (area/perimeter). Needs to consolidate his conceptual understanding of adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators. Target: Secure the method for finding the lowest common multiple (LCM) when working with fractions.
English: A charismatic and highly persuasive speaker. Understands how to manipulate an audience during speech writing. Needs to translate his fantastic verbal arguments into highly structured, formally written paragraphs. Target: Consistently use the PEE (Point, Evidence, Explain) structure to organize analytical reading responses.
Creative Arts: Oliver is a very popular boy who naturally takes the lead in group plays and isn't afraid to perform. In Music, he is great at playing by ear or tapping out a rhythm, but he completely tunes out when we try to teach him how to read basic sheet music. His target is to learn how to read just a few basic musical notes so he isn't relying entirely on his memory and hearing.
PE: Oliver is very charming and loves to look the part in his brand new trainers, but he avoids breaking a sweat at all costs! During cross-country or football, he will linger at the back of the pack, chatting with his friends and actively avoiding the ball. He could do better in Year 7 if he is placed in a small-sided game (like 3v3 basketball) where there is nowhere to hide, forcing him to engage physically.
Sciences: Oliver is very confident and asks brilliant, out-of-the-box "what if" questions about space and gravity! During group work, he relies entirely on his charm, letting his lab partner do all the actual measuring, pouring, and recording while he just watches. His target is to take on the strict role of "equipment manager" for at least one half-term, forcing him to do the physical measuring and data collection himself.
Humanities: Oliver is a very charming boy who can talk his way out of anything! He will give a brilliant, persuasive speech during a History debate about the suffragettes, but when it comes time to actually write the essay, he will chat with his friends and write two sentences. He could do better if he is not allowed to share his brilliant verbal ideas with the class until he has physically written down three bullet points summarizing his argument.

Transition Portrait: Toby is the kind of student who flies entirely under the radar. He is unfailingly polite, consistently completes his work to a good standard, and never causes a moment of trouble. However, he is incredibly introverted and rarely volunteers an answer. Teachers need to make a conscious effort to build a relationship with him, as he will not initiate contact.
Home Life & Interests: Toby lives with his parents in a very quiet, structured home. He is an avid gamer and spends the vast majority of his free time playing single-player RPG (Role Playing Games) video games. He prefers the predictable, structured worlds of his games to the noisy, unpredictable nature of socializing with large groups of peers.
Maths: Perfectly capable of meeting the expected standard independently; shows strong mental math when focused and engaged. Attempts to remain completely invisible. Will sit in silence for an entire lesson rather than ask for help if he gets stuck. Target: Establish a low-stakes communication method (e.g., leaving a post-it note on the corner of the desk) to ask for help without drawing attention.
English: Actually possesses a fantastic, high-level vocabulary (picked up from fantasy RPG gaming) and reads well independently. Writes the absolute bare minimum required to avoid drawing attention to himself. Never volunteers to read aloud or share ideas. Target: Use the "Because, But, So" sentence scaffold to force him to expand on his very brief, single-sentence answers.
Creative Arts: Toby will do absolutely everything in his power to be invisible during Drama and Music; he will literally hide behind the taller boys to avoid being seen! He has a great imagination (mostly from his video games) but refuses to act it out. His target for Year 7 is to be the "foley artist" for a play, creating the sound effects from behind a screen or curtain so he can join in without being looked at.
PE: Toby is our final absolute refuser. He hates getting sweaty, hates the noise of the sports hall, and will do everything in his power to hide in the changing rooms or claim he has a twisted ankle to avoid participating. He could do much better if his transition to PE includes a high-tech alternative role, such as using an iPad to film his team's performance for tactical analysis, keeping him involved in the lesson without forcing him onto the pitch.
Sciences: Toby actually knows a lot of science from watching YouTube videos, but he will do everything in his power to hide at the back of the lab. He refuses to answer questions, wear the safety goggles, or take the lead on a practical, preferring to just watch silently. His target is to be assigned the role of the "digital data inputter," giving him a quiet, screen-based task that keeps him involved without being in the spotlight.
Humanities: Toby actually knows a lot of history from playing strategy video games, but he will do everything in his power to hide at the back of the room and write the absolute bare minimum required. He refuses to expand on his answers. He would improve if his teacher uses "Because, But, So" sentence scaffolds to force him to expand on his brief, single-sentence historical summaries.

Transition Portrait: Willow is a quintessential "under the radar" student; she is quiet, compliant, always does exactly what is asked of her, but never volunteers an answer. She makes steady progress but rarely pushes herself out of her comfort zone. Teachers need to actively target her with questioning to ensure she is engaging with the material and not just copying off the board.
Home Life & Interests: Willow lives with her mum and two younger brothers, whom she helps care for in the evenings while mum works a second job. Because she is so responsible at home, she tends to retreat into herself at school to conserve energy. She enjoys quiet, solitary hobbies like diamond painting and watching baking shows.
Maths: A quiet, highly independent learner who has solidly met the expected standard, particularly in calculating area and perimeter. Flies completely under the radar. Rarely pushes herself to tackle Greater Depth challenges unless explicitly prompted. Target: Independently select and attempt at least one Greater Depth/Extension challenge per topic unit.
English: Consistently meets the expected standard in all areas. A quiet, reliable, and highly independent worker. Flies completely under the radar. Rarely pushes herself to use more ambitious vocabulary or complex sentence structures. Target: Actively incorporate at least two pieces of newly learned "challenge" vocabulary into every piece of extended writing.
Creative Arts: Willow is a lovely, quiet girl who just gets on with her work without making any fuss. She colors in nicely and sings along quietly, but she flies completely under the radar and never volunteers for a solo or tries a hard drawing technique. Her target is to put her hand up and volunteer for just one "challenge" task a term, like trying to mix a difficult paint color or taking a lead acting role.
PE: Willow is a completely reliable student; she always wears the correct kit, jogs around the pitch quietly, and never causes a fuss. She completely coasts, however, barely breaking a sweat and actively avoiding the ball so she doesn't have to do any real work. She could do better if she is explicitly challenged to take a minimum of five shots or make five tackles per game to ensure she is actively contributing to the lesson.
Sciences: Willow is a quiet, reliable girl who will happily copy the results table off the board and color it in nicely. She coasts through group work, though, letting her louder partner do all the fun stuff like lighting the Bunsen burner or looking through the microscope. Her target is to step up and take the physical lead on at least one piece of technical lab equipment every half-term.
Humanities: Willow is the definition of a reliable student. She copies the Geography diagrams perfectly, writes neat History sentences, and never causes a fuss. She completely coasts, however, and will never pick the "challenge" question or offer her own opinion in RE. She could do better by actively choosing the hardest worksheet or the extension task at least once a fortnight to prove to herself what she is actually capable of.

Transition Portrait: Ava is an exceptional problem solver with a razor-sharp intellect, particularly in STEM subjects. Although English is her second language, she is fully fluent and writes with an impressive vocabulary, though she sometimes struggles with local colloquialisms. She is fiercely competitive and thrives in debate or logic-based tasks.
Home Life & Interests: Ava's parents moved to Plymouth from Poland before she was born, and the family is deeply embedded in the local Polish community. She is proudly bilingual and attends a Saturday school to maintain her written Polish. At home, she plays chess competitively with her father and travels regionally for tournaments.
Maths: Working comfortably at Greater Depth (GDS) across all areas. Outstanding mental agility and constructs rigorous mathematical proofs. Can be highly self-critical. Occasionally over-complicates straightforward arithmetic by expecting a "trick" question. Target: Act as a peer-tutor to practice explaining complex methods in the simplest, most accessible terms.
English: Highly sophisticated reading comprehension. Analyzes layers of meaning and subtext with maturity. Self-critical perfectionism leads her to over-edit her creative writing until it loses its natural voice and flow. Target: Practice timed, unstructured "free-writing" where crossing out and editing are strictly banned.
Creative Arts: Ava is brilliant at understanding the emotions in a story, but she is such a perfectionist. If her painting doesn't look exactly like the picture in her head, she gets incredibly frustrated and has been known to rip pages out of her sketchbook. Her target for Year 7 is to try abstract art or finger-painting, where things are supposed to look messy, to help her let go of needing everything to be perfect.
PE: Ava has great natural rhythm and enjoys choreographed dance and aerobics. She is such an over-thinker, though, that during fast-paced invasion games she will freeze with the ball, analyzing three different passing options until she is inevitably tackled. She could do better if she practices fast-paced, high-pressure drills where she is forced to make a split-second decision and pass the ball within two seconds of receiving it.
Sciences: Ava is brilliant at understanding complex scientific concepts, but her perfectionism makes graphing a nightmare. If her results don't form a perfectly straight line, she gets incredibly frustrated and assumes she has ruined the whole experiment. Her target for Year 7 is to learn about "anomalous results" and the "line of best fit," understanding that real-world science is rarely mathematically perfect.
Humanities: Ava is a very deep thinker and writes incredibly sophisticated answers when analyzing historical sources. She is such an over-thinker, though, that she will panic if a source is biased or contradictory, assuming she has misunderstood the entire lesson. She could do better by trusting her first instinct, explicitly practicing how to write sentences like "This source is unreliable because..." to build her confidence in critiquing adults from the past.

Transition Portrait: Riley uses humor as a defense mechanism to deflect from his academic insecurities. If he doesn't understand a task, he will crack a joke to distract the teacher rather than asking for help. He responds terribly to public reprimands but will work incredibly hard for a teacher who acknowledges his humor privately while holding firm on academic expectations.
Home Life & Interests: Riley lives with his nan, who adores him but finds his cheeky attitude exhausting at times. His main outlet is skateboarding; he spends hours at the local skatepark trying to master complex tricks, showing incredible physical resilience when he falls. If he applied his skateboarding dedication to his studies, he would soar.
Maths: Underlying grasp of number operations is solid. Loves competitive, verbal, game-based mental math starters. Diagnosed dysgraphia means the physical act of writing is exhausting. Presentation is chaotic, and numbers are frequently illegible, leading to place-value errors. Uses humor to avoid written tasks. Target: Remove the physical barrier of writing by allowing him to complete extended calculation practice on a tablet or by providing pre-printed, oversized calculation grids.
English: A brilliant verbal storyteller. Generates highly creative, funny, and engaging narrative ideas during class discussions. Dysgraphia makes the physical act of writing painful and exhausting. Handwriting is illegible, and he uses classroom clowning to avoid it. Target: Completely remove the physical barrier by allowing him to type all extended writing or use speech-to-text software.
Creative Arts: Because of his dysgraphia, Riley can't write down a script, but he is absolutely hilarious at improvised comedy! In art, though, he tends to make a massive mess with the paint on purpose just to make the boys at his table laugh. His target is to channel his comedy into a planned, rehearsed scene rather than just goofing around to distract people.
PE: Riley's dysgraphia means he loves the physical release of PE, but he uses it as an excuse to play the class clown! He will do dramatic, fake slow-motion dives during football or pretend to fall over in gymnastics just to make the boys laugh, completely disrupting the lesson. He could do better if he is challenged to channel that performative energy into choreographing a serious, structured parkour or gymnastics routine where he has to demonstrate actual control.
Sciences: Riley's dysgraphia means writing out a long scientific method is painful and exhausting. To avoid the writing, he plays the class clown, purposefully making a massive, bubbly mess with his experiment to make the boys at his table laugh. His target is to completely remove the writing barrier by allowing him to verbally record his scientific conclusion on a tablet.
Humanities: Riley's dysgraphia makes writing extended History essays incredibly painful and exhausting. To avoid the writing, he plays the class clown, acting out silly historical deaths to make the boys at his table laugh. He could do much better if the physical barrier of writing is completely removed by allowing him to type all of his Humanities essays on a laptop or use speech-to-text software.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Irlen Syndrome & Dyslexia. Uses a blue overlay and size 14+ font.
Transition Portrait: Rosie is a quiet, creative student who tries very hard to mask her reading difficulties, often pretending to read along with the class. When faced with dense, unformatted text, she experiences visual stress and will engage in work avoidance, such as asking to use the toilet. Ensuring all resources are printed on blue paper transforms her ability to engage.
Home Life & Interests: Rosie lives with her mum and dad, who are strong advocates for her SEN needs and ensure she has all her colored overlays and specialized equipment. She has a fantastic eye for detail and loves photography, spending weekends taking macro photos of insects and flowers in local parks.
Maths: Reads data, line graphs, and charts accurately when using her tinted Irlen overlays. Highly creative thinker. Needs to consolidate her formal written methods for long multiplication and division, as numbers can "jump" on the page. Target: Consistently advocate for and use pale blue colored overlays or paper for all dense number grids and assessments.
English: Extremely imaginative; her creative writing features vivid, highly unique worlds and character concepts. Irlen syndrome makes reading standard black-on-white text fatiguing, causing the words to "jump" and leading to missed comprehension details. Target: Consistently advocate for and use pale blue overlays or blue printed paper for all reading comprehension tasks and assessments.
Creative Arts: Rosie draws the most amazing, magical fantasy worlds and loves designing costumes for our plays! Because of her Irlen syndrome, reading sheet music or scripts printed on white paper makes her eyes hurt, so she often gives up. Her target for Year 7 is to make sure she always advocates for herself by asking the teacher for her pale blue overlay before starting any reading task in Music or Drama.
PE: Rosie is a wonderful dancer and loves indoor gymnastics! Because of her Irlen syndrome, the bright glare of the sun on an outdoor field or the stark white lines on a sports hall floor can cause visual stress and headaches, making it hard for her to track a fast-moving ball. She could do better if she is allowed to wear tinted sports glasses outdoors, and if the teacher uses brightly colored (e.g., neon orange) balls that are easier for her to track.
Sciences: Rosie is wonderfully imaginative and loves learning about space and the planets. Because of her Irlen syndrome, trying to read the tiny, tightly packed numbers on the side of a glass measuring cylinder or a thermometer makes the numbers "jump," leading to inaccurate results. Her target is to always work with a partner to double-check her scale readings before writing them down.
Humanities: Rosie writes the most wonderfully creative and vivid descriptions of historical settings! Because of her Irlen syndrome, looking at complex, detailed Ordnance Survey maps or reading tiny text in a historical atlas makes the lines blur and gives her a headache, so she avoids Geography. She could do better by always using her pale blue overlay when doing map work, and using digital, zoomable maps on an iPad where possible.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed ADHD (Combined Type). Medicates at 8 AM; focus drops sharply by afternoon lessons.
Transition Portrait: Grace is incredibly creative, funny, and full of bright ideas, but getting those ideas down on paper is a daily struggle. Her concentration is relatively good in the morning following her medication but falls off a cliff after lunch, leading to fidgeting and impulsivity. Breaking tasks down into 10-minute chunks helps her maintain momentum.
Home Life & Interests: Grace lives with her mum and stepdad in a busy household. She is obsessed with social media, specifically TikTok, and spends hours learning complex dance routines in her bedroom. Her parents monitor her phone usage closely, as she has previously struggled with online peer drama that spilled over into the school day.
Maths: Offers brilliant, intuitive verbal reasoning during class discussions and mental math starters. Written calculations completely lack a systematic layout, leading to frequent, careless errors in arithmetic papers. Target: Show clear, step-by-step working out for every calculation, regardless of how "easy" the question appears.
English: Brings great energy to collaborative group work. Excels at writing lively, authentic dialogue in narratives. Informal "text-speak" and slang frequently bleed into her academic writing. Often forgets to use Standard English. Target: Actively edit and "upgrade" vocabulary, replacing informal slang with formal academic register during the drafting phase.
Creative Arts: Grace loves drama, especially if it involves acting out TikTok trends or creating little videos with her friends! She does have a very short attention span, though, and gets bored of practicing a scene, wanting to change the idea every five minutes. Her target is to stick with just one drama idea for a whole lesson and practice it three times without changing the plot.
PE: Grace loves PE when it involves chatting with her friends in the outfield during rounders or cricket! She has very little focus on the actual sport, often missing catches because she is facing the wrong way or talking about her weekend. She could do better if she is put into fast-paced rotational games where she has to constantly change positions, preventing her from getting too comfortable and distracted.
Sciences: Grace loves group work and chatting about animals, but she has a very short attention span for the actual scientific process. If she has to wait five minutes for a beaker of water to heat up or a chemical to dissolve, she will wander off to talk to her friends. Her target is to be given a highly specific, timed job during experiments—such as "record the temperature every 30 seconds with a stopwatch"—to keep her anchored to her desk.
Humanities: Grace loves learning about social history, especially what life was like for Victorian children or what clothes people wore in the 1960s! She gets incredibly bored with physical Geography, though, and will zone out entirely when we learn about the water cycle or rock formations. She could do better by working in short, highly focused bursts during physical Geography, breaking up the "boring" facts with quick quizzes to keep her anchored.

Transition Portrait: Samuel is a highly logical, scientific thinker who loves conducting experiments and testing hypotheses. He is very black-and-white in his worldview; he likes definitive answers and can become frustrated in subjects like English or RS where interpretation is required. He thrives when teachers explain the precise 'why' behind a rule or concept.
Home Life & Interests: Samuel lives with his parents and is an avid collector and categorizer. His current obsession is paleontology; he spends his weekends combing the Jurassic Coast for fossils and has a meticulously labeled collection in his bedroom. His parents foster this intellectual curiosity by taking him to museums and science lectures.
Maths: Thinks like a scientist. Exceptional ability to organize, present, and interpret discrete and continuous data using graphs. Can be highly rigid. Struggles to accept that there can be multiple valid methods to arrive at the same mathematical answer. Target: Practice comparing two different calculation methods for the same problem and identifying the merits of both.
English: Structures non-fiction arguments and reports with highly logical, precise, and factual vocabulary. Highly literal thinker. Struggles to analyze poetry or write creatively, as he views fiction as "illogical." Target: Explicitly practice decoding metaphors by treating them as logical "codes" or puzzles to be solved.
Creative Arts: Samuel is very logical and treats art like a math problem. If asked to draw a house, he will use a ruler for every single line and get upset if it isn't perfectly straight. He completely refuses to play "silly" drama games about feelings. His target is to design the set or handle the lighting for a play, using his technical skills instead of forcing him to act.
PE: Samuel is highly logical and expects the sports pitch to be perfectly geometric. He gets very frustrated during outdoor sports if the grass is muddy, the cones aren't in a perfectly straight line, or if the referee makes a subjective call that he views as mathematically incorrect. He would improve if he is encouraged to participate in highly structured, measurable sports like track and field, where the rules and results are absolute.
Sciences: Samuel is highly logical and expects science to be perfectly exact. If he is measuring the boiling point of water and his thermometer says 99°C instead of 100°C, he will get highly frustrated and insist the equipment is broken. His target for Year 7 is to learn about "variables" and "margins of error," understanding that the real world is slightly messier than a textbook.
Humanities: Samuel is highly logical and expects Geography to be perfectly exact. He gets very frustrated in History because historical sources often contradict each other, and he views Religious Education as completely illogical. He could do better in Year 7 by approaching RE and History purely through an anthropological lens, studying the "cause and effect" of human beliefs and actions rather than focusing on emotion.

Transition Portrait: William is a profoundly gifted mathematician who grasps abstract concepts with a speed that is sometimes startling. He is genuinely enthusiastic about learning and frequently asks highly advanced questions that go beyond the curriculum. He must be heavily challenged with top-tier extension work, otherwise, he will finish his tasks in five minutes and become bored.
Home Life & Interests: William's parents are highly ambitious for him and provide a very enriching home environment. He receives private piano tuition and has reached Grade 6, and he also spends time building complex coding projects online. Despite his obvious intellect, he remains very grounded and has a lovely, self-deprecating sense of humor.
Maths: A profound mathematical prodigy working significantly beyond the KS2 curriculum. Intuitively understands complex algebraic theorems. Can become bored, disconnected, or socially isolated from peers when forced to complete standard curriculum tasks. Target: Engage with KS3/KS4 extension materials (e.g., UKMT Junior Math Challenge past papers) alongside core lessons.
English: Reads significantly above age level. His analytical writing is incredibly mature, structured, and academic. Quickly becomes bored, disconnected, and sometimes disruptive when studying standard KS2 texts he considers too simple. Target: Provide KS3/KS4 level extension texts or critical essays alongside the standard class reader to maintain his intellectual engagement.
Creative Arts: William is incredibly smart and actually plays the piano at a Grade 5 level outside of school. Because he is so advanced, he gets really grumpy and sighs loudly when we have to do basic xylophone lessons or simple drama games, which upsets the other children. To stop him from getting bored, his target is to be given a harder piece of sheet music or asked to compose his own background music for the class plays.
PE: William is profoundly gifted academically and views PE as a complete waste of his intellectual time. He will often sigh loudly, walk instead of run, and complain that chasing a football is "pointless." He would improve if he is given a leadership role, such as acting as the team coach, where he has to use his intellect to analyze the opposing team's weaknesses and devise tactical plays for his peers to execute.
Sciences: William is incredibly smart and already knows most of the KS3 science curriculum from reading encyclopedias at home. He gets very grumpy and sighs loudly when we do basic primary experiments like floating and sinking, which upsets the other children. To maintain his engagement, his target is to be given extension questions that ask him to explain the abstract why behind the science, rather than just the basic what.
Humanities: William is profoundly gifted and reads adult history books at home for fun. He views the Year 6 curriculum on the Egyptians or basic river features as insulting to his intelligence, occasionally refusing to work or correcting the teacher's minor factual errors loudly. He could do better if he is provided with KS3 or even GCSE-level historical sources (like real political cartoons or complex data) to analyze so he remains intellectually engaged.

Transition Portrait: Zachary is a highly energetic boy who lacks a filter and frequently calls out or wanders around the room if he is bored. He is not malicious, but he needs a firm, fair hand to keep him on track. He responds incredibly well to teachers who take an interest in his sports and use a "tough love" approach combined with high-praise when he succeeds.
Home Life & Interests: Zachary lives with his mum, who works long hours, meaning he has a lot of unstructured free time. He is a phenomenal basketball player and spends most evenings at the local courts playing pick-up games with older teenagers. He looks up to the older boys and occasionally mimics their bravado, which can get him into trouble at school.
Maths: Highly confident mathematician who races through arithmetic papers. Excellent spatial awareness and understanding of geometry. Rushes work to finish first, leading to careless errors. Easily bored and will distract others if not challenged immediately. Target: Independently transition to Greater Depth extension tasks to maintain engagement and reduce disruptive behavior once core work is finished.
English: Brings great, confident energy to class discussions. Highly engaged by fast-paced, action-oriented narratives and sports biographies. Skim-reads texts, frequently missing crucial, subtle details. Handwriting and spelling deteriorate rapidly because he is rushing to finish. Target: Stop at the end of every single paragraph during extended writing to deliberately proofread for missing words and basic punctuation.
Creative Arts: Zachary does everything at 100 miles per hour! He will slap three colors of paint on a page in two minutes and shout "Done!", and his group plays usually consist of him rushing through his lines in ten seconds so he can sit back down. His target is to perform a short scene entirely in slow-motion, forcing him to actually think about his body language and pace.
PE: Zachary does absolutely everything at a full sprint! He has incredible initial speed but zero pacing, meaning he will sprint the first 100 meters of a cross-country race and then completely collapse, exhausted, for the rest of the lesson. He could do better if he is taught how to pace his physical output, perhaps by being required to run behind a designated "pacer" student who controls the speed of the group.
Sciences: Zachary does everything at 100 miles per hour! He will dump all the chemicals together in ten seconds and then wonder why his experiment didn't work, completely ignoring the instruction to "stir slowly and observe." His target is to work with a physical sand-timer on his desk, forcing him to wait a full minute between each step of the practical method to slow his impulses down.
Humanities: Zachary does everything at top speed! He will color a Geography map in thirty seconds, scribbling wildly over the borders, and his History essays are usually unfinished because he is rushing to go out to play. He could do better if he is required to swap his work with a peer and complete a "quality check" checklist before he is allowed to pack away.

Transition Portrait: Florence is a natural leader with a commanding presence and an incredibly articulate way of expressing herself. She is highly empathetic, often stepping in to defend classmates if she feels an injustice has occurred. She excels in humanities and English, where she can debate and discuss morality, history, and social issues with maturity beyond her years.
Home Life & Interests: Florence lives with her mum, who encourages her to be vocal and stand up for her beliefs. She has a beautiful singing voice and is a leading member of a prestigious local youth choir, performing regularly across the city. Her involvement in the choir has given her excellent discipline and public speaking skills.
Maths: A commanding presence in group tasks. Has an excellent understanding of prime numbers, factors, and multiples. Can dominate group problem-solving tasks, occasionally preventing quieter peers from contributing their ideas. Target: Actively solicit and validate mathematical ideas from quieter group members during collaborative investigations.
English: A natural leader in group drama tasks. Writes highly effective and engaging persuasive speeches. Can completely dominate group work, talking over peers and insisting her interpretation of a text is the only correct one. Target: Practice active listening skills by accurately summarizing a peer's viewpoint before sharing her own during discussions.
Creative Arts: Florence is definitely the boss! She is great at organizing people, but in group drama tasks, she will literally push her friends into their stage positions and tell everyone exactly how to say their lines. Her target for Year 7 is to practice zipping her lips and letting someone else be the "director" for the day, practicing listening to other people's ideas without interrupting them.
PE: Florence is a natural leader and a fantastic netball player! She is incredibly bossy, however, and will try to dictate exactly where everyone on her team should stand, often shouting at her friends if they don't follow her instructions. She could do better in Year 7 if she is explicitly banned from talking during the first half of a match, forcing her to rely purely on non-verbal communication and trust her teammates' decisions.
Sciences: Florence is a fantastic organizer, but she completely takes over during science experiments! She will measure the liquids, light the burner, conduct the test, and write down the results, leaving her partner standing there with absolutely nothing to do. Her target for Year 7 is to practice strict turn-taking; she can set up the equipment, but she must sit on her hands and let her partner actually conduct the test.
Humanities: Florence is a fantastic project manager, but during group Geography projects (like building a model of the water cycle), she will snatch the materials out of her friends' hands and do the whole thing herself because she wants it done "right." She could do better in Year 7 by taking on the strict role of "Project Director" where she is only allowed to give verbal instructions and must keep her hands strictly behind her back.

Transition Portrait: Harry is a dependable, solid all-rounder who consistently hands in good quality work without making a fuss. He is incredibly popular among his peers due to his fair and easygoing nature, often acting as a mediator when playground arguments occur. He could be pushed to contribute more in class discussions, as he often lets louder voices take over.
Home Life & Interests: Harry enjoys a very stable and active home life with his parents, who prioritize outdoor experiences over screen time. The family owns a campervan and spends most weekends exploring Dartmoor or the Cornish coast.
