Because of this, Harry has a great appreciation for nature and is practically minded, enjoying bushcraft and hiking.
Maths: Applies math superbly to real-world contexts, particularly in measurement, volume, and converting metric units. Needs to actively practice and secure his formal written methods for long multiplication and division. Target: Consolidate the formal column method for long multiplication, specifically when multiplying decimals.
English: Excellent at transactional and instructional writing (e.g., recounting a DofE expedition or writing a survival guide). Vocabulary in fictional creative writing can be quite basic, repetitive, and heavily reliant on simple adjectives. Target: Independently use a thesaurus to "upgrade" at least three basic adjectives per paragraph in creative writing.
Creative Arts: Harry loves being outside and his best artwork usually involves drawing animals, trees, or bugs. He finds drama and music a bit embarrassing and prefers to hide at the back of the group, usually volunteering to hold the props. His target for Year 7 is to slowly build his confidence by just saying one or two loud, clear lines in a group play.
PE: Harry is in his element when he is outdoors; he loves cross-country, rugby, and doesn't care if it is freezing cold or pouring with rain! He is quite uncoordinated when it comes to fine motor sports, like serving in tennis or hitting a cricket ball, often getting frustrated and hitting the equipment. He would improve if he practices breaking down complex movements into slow, deliberate practice swings before trying to hit a moving ball.
Sciences: Harry absolutely shines in biology and forest school; he doesn't mind getting muddy to look at worms or study plants! He struggles to care about abstract chemistry or physics, like dissolving powders or learning about light, because he can't see the point. His target is to explicitly link those abstract concepts back to the real world, such as learning about filtering by filtering dirty mud-water.
Humanities: Harry really shone on our Geography field trip; he didn't mind getting muddy to measure the stream and loved using the compasses. He finds RE and empathetic History writing very "cringe" and will usually just write "I don't know" to avoid engaging with his emotions. He could do better by linking RE topics back to his love of the outdoors, focusing heavily on topics like environmental stewardship, creation, and animal rights.

Transition Portrait: Isabella is a fiercely ambitious, highly driven student who demands excellence from herself in every subject. She has exceptional presentation skills and is incredibly confident speaking in front of large audiences or leading assemblies. She occasionally needs guidance on how to manage her time, as she tends to over-commit to extracurriculars.
Home Life & Interests: Isabella's parents moved to the UK from Nigeria and instill a profound respect for education and success in their children. Isabella is highly cultured, speaks three languages fluently (English, Yoruba, and French), and is politically engaged. She expects to go to a top university and works relentlessly toward that goal.
Maths: Working comfortably at Greater Depth. Solves sophisticated ratio and algebra problems intuitively and with high confidence. Her drive to be the "best" means she frequently performs complex calculations mentally and refuses to document her methods. Target: Must write down clear, sequential working out to secure the essential method marks in KS3 assessments.
English: Writes highly commanding persuasive texts. Vocabulary is significantly above age-related expectations. Her fierce ambition means she refuses to accept critical feedback; she views her first drafts as flawless and final. Target: Engage in structured peer-assessment to learn how to accept and apply constructive criticism to her own work.
Creative Arts: Isabella is fiercely competitive and will actually argue with the teacher if she doesn't get the main speaking part in the school play! In art, if her painting isn't "the best" on the table, she gets very cross with herself. Her target is to practice being in the chorus or the ensemble, learning that the background roles are just as important as the lead.
PE: Isabella is fiercely competitive and views every single PE lesson as the Olympic finals! She is a highly skilled footballer but a terrible loser, frequently storming off the pitch or blaming her teammates if they concede a goal. She could do much better in Year 7 if she is explicitly taught how to lose gracefully, perhaps by being required to shake hands and offer a compliment to the opposing team after every match.
Sciences: Isabella is fiercely competitive and treats every practical like a contest. If her group's paper airplane doesn't fly the furthest, or her copper sulfate crystal isn't the biggest, she will argue that the experiment was unfair! Her target is to practice evaluating why an experiment failed, learning that in science, a "failed" experiment is still useful data.
Humanities: Isabella is fiercely competitive and treats class debates in RE or History as arguments she has to win. If someone challenges her interpretation of a historical event, she will argue back forcefully and refuse to accept any other viewpoint. She would improve if she regularly participates in "devil's advocate" exercises, where she is forced to research and argue the opposite of her actual opinion to build cognitive flexibility.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Spina Bifida. Full-time wheelchair user. Requires accessible desk and personal care support.
Transition Portrait: Amelia is a remarkably resilient and diligent student who approaches every task with a positive mindset. She advocates well for her own physical needs but never wants her disability to be the focus of her identity in the classroom. She works well with everyone and is particularly strong at organizing collaborative tasks and keeping her peers on track.
Home Life & Interests: Amelia lives with her parents and two older sisters in a fully adapted home. She is fiercely independent and incredibly active in the disabled sports community, particularly enjoying adaptive rowing on the weekends. Her family is highly supportive, ensuring she attends various clubs, which has given her a wonderful sense of self-confidence.
Maths: Incredibly resilient problem-solver. Thrives on challenging ratio and proportion tasks and works systematically. Can sometimes over-complicate simple arithmetic by looking for a "trick" where there isn't one. Target: Practice quickly identifying when a mental math strategy is more efficient than a formal written method.
English: Highly articulate. Excels in speaking and listening tasks, debates, and structuring logical, non-fiction reports. Can be overly pragmatic and literal in her creative writing, lacking descriptive flair and emotional engagement. Target: Consciously incorporate sensory language (sight, sound, texture) to elevate setting descriptions in creative tasks.
Creative Arts: Amelia is wonderfully loud and confident—she loves being front and center in Drama and has a great speaking voice! Sometimes in Music, she gets frustrated if her wheelchair makes it tricky to quickly reach different instruments like the keyboards or drum kits. Our target to make Year 7 smooth for her is to make sure the teacher sets up an easily reachable instrument station for her before the lesson starts.
PE: Amelia is incredibly enthusiastic and has a fantastic understanding of game rules, making her an excellent tactician in adapted sports like boccia or wheelchair basketball! In outdoor lessons on grass, her wheelchair can get bogged down, which leaves her feeling isolated on the sidelines. She could do better if the teacher ensures that all outdoor team games are heavily modified so she can play a central, active role, such as being the permanent center-pass in netball.
Sciences: Amelia is very logical and particularly enjoys learning about the human body, muscles, and keeping healthy! Sometimes in a busy classroom, she can struggle to reach high sinks or standard workbenches in her wheelchair, which frustrates her independence. Her target to ensure a smooth transition to a secondary science lab is to have a designated, lowered workspace with a clear pathway to the safety equipment.
Humanities: Amelia is incredibly articulate and shines during RE debates and History source analysis, arguing her points with real logic. Sometimes, outdoor Geography fieldwork (like measuring river flow in the local woods) can be frustrating if the terrain isn't perfectly wheelchair accessible, leaving her feeling left out. She could do better during fieldwork if the teacher assigns her the crucial role of "Chief Data Analyst," ensuring she is the central hub of the group's outdoor learning.

Transition Portrait: Elsie is a dreamer with a wonderful imagination, which translates into beautifully creative piece of narrative writing. However, she is highly disorganized; her bag is a black hole, she frequently loses worksheets, and she struggles to keep track of deadlines. She needs regular support with executive functioning, such as checking her planner at the end of the day.
Home Life & Interests: Elsie lives with her mum, who works in the creative arts, and their home is filled with books, music, and art supplies. Elsie spends hours writing her own short stories and poetry, often illustrating them herself. While her home environment is very loving, it is fairly unstructured, which contributes to her disorganization at school.
Maths: Frequently generates highly creative, out-of-the-box solutions to open-ended math investigations. Highly disorganized written layout means she frequently loses track of her own calculations mid-problem. Target: Keep all working out strictly aligned within the margins of the page, utilizing grid squares for place value.
English: Generates highly original, imaginative, and "out-of-the-box" ideas during creative writing brainstorming. Written presentation is chaotic. Narratives often lack any logical paragraph structure, making them difficult to follow. Target: Use a physical "paragraph planner" template to map out changes in time, place, or topic (TiPToP) before writing.
Creative Arts: Elsie has the wildest imagination—she usually wants her group plays to feature time travel, aliens, and a dance battle all in two minutes! Her execution is totally chaotic, though; she makes a massive mess with paint and her plays never actually make sense. Her target for Year 7 is to use a simple three-box comic strip to plan a clear beginning, middle, and end before she's allowed to start acting or painting.
PE: Elsie has a wild imagination and treats PE like a giant game of make-believe! During a serious game of netball, she will suddenly decide she is a ninja and start doing dramatic rolls across the court, completely abandoning her position and confusing everyone. She could do better if she is encouraged to channel that imagination into choreographing highly dramatic gymnastics routines, while practicing strict positional discipline in team sports.
Sciences: Elsie treats science like making a magical potion! She has a wildly chaotic approach to practicals and, if left unsupervised, will try to mix every chemical on the desk together just to see what happens. Her target for Year 7 is to stick strictly to the provided equipment list, checking off each item as she uses it to keep her focused on the actual experiment rather than just playing.
Humanities: Elsie's imagination is wild! When asked to write a factual report on the Great Fire of London, she basically wrote a fantasy novel featuring dragons and magic! She has absolutely no sense of chronology and mixes up the Romans with WWII constantly. She could do better in Year 7 by creating a physical, visual timeline on her desk, and pointing to the correct century before she is allowed to start writing anything.

Transition Portrait: Isaac is a genuine high-flyer who absorbs knowledge rapidly and thinks deeply about abstract concepts. He is never satisfied with simply finishing a task; he wants to know how to take it to the next level and constantly requests extension work. He has a brilliant, inquisitive mind but can become frustrated if lessons move too slowly for him.
Home Life & Interests: Isaac lives with his parents, who provide a highly stimulating environment filled with books and documentaries. He is fascinated by space and astrophysics; he owns a high-powered telescope and spends clear nights mapping constellations. He hopes to study physics at a top university and is already researching career paths in astronomy.
Maths: Approaches math with the intensity of a physicist. Working at Greater Depth, seamlessly applying complex algebraic formulas. Can rush through basic arithmetic because his brain is already three steps ahead, leading to silly sign (+/-) errors. Target: Implement a structured checking mechanism for all arithmetic papers to completely eliminate easily avoidable calculation errors.
English: Reads complex, adult-level non-fiction effortlessly. Has an incredibly vast, highly technical vocabulary. Highly resistant to analyzing fiction or poetry; gets frustrated by ambiguity and texts where there isn't one strict, "correct" interpretation. Target: Explicitly practice exploring "multiple interpretations" of a quote, actively finding two different ways a character's dialogue could be read.
Creative Arts: Isaac plays the keyboard technically perfectly, but he plays it a bit like a robot! He hates anything in Art or Drama that doesn't have a strict, logical rule, and gets very frustrated when asked to "just express himself." His target for Year 7 is to improvise a short, four-bar melody completely from his head, without being allowed to look at any sheet music.
PE: Isaac has great stamina but moves in a very robotic, stiff manner. He excels at highly repetitive sports like rowing or swimming, but he hates expressive dance or gymnastics, finding the lack of a numerical score completely pointless and frustrating. He would improve if expressive movement is framed to him as a biomechanical exercise, focusing on muscle control, balance, and physical strength rather than artistic expression.
Sciences: Isaac is brilliant at the mathematical side of science, like calculating averages or drawing perfect graphs. He gets very frustrated, however, if an experiment involves subjective observations like "what shade of blue is the liquid?" or if there isn't a strict numerical answer. His target is to practice recording qualitative data, focusing on describing what he sees and smells rather than just writing down numbers.
Humanities: Isaac loves the data-driven side of Geography, like analyzing climate graphs. He gets very frustrated by the subjectivity of Religious Education and empathetic History, disliking topics where there isn't one strict, "correct" numerical answer. He would improve if he is encouraged to approach subjective Humanities topics as "data collection," gathering different viewpoints like survey results rather than searching for one absolute truth.

Transition Portrait: Sienna is an incredibly sweet-natured, gentle girl who can be far too passive in her own learning journey. She is happy to sit quietly at the back of the room, copy off the board, and go unnoticed by the teacher. Staff must actively draw her into discussions and provide structured opportunities for her to share her opinions.
Home Life & Interests: Sienna lives with her mum and is very close to her cousins, who live nearby. She is obsessed with pop music, K-Pop, and learning the associated choreographies, which she practices with her friends. She is very image-conscious and deeply values her peer group's opinions above all else.
Maths: A quiet, capable learner who is highly secure in translating, reflecting, and plotting coordinates across four quadrants. Very passive. Tends to give up immediately on 3-mark reasoning questions if the first step isn't perfectly clear. Target: Build academic stamina by identifying and completing just the first operational step of multi-mark word problems.
English: Very neat presentation and follows instructions perfectly; enjoys analyzing modern media, pop culture, and lyrics. Extremely passive in lessons; will rarely offer an opinion on a text unless she is directly called upon by the teacher. Target: Prepare one "wondering" question about the class text to ask the teacher or a peer at the start of the lesson.
Creative Arts: Sienna is very passive. If her friends decide they are drawing cats, she will draw a cat. If they decide they are acting out a robbery, she will just nod and go along with it. She rarely comes up with her own creative ideas and just waits to be told what to do. Her target is to be the one who chooses the theme or the starting idea for her group's project at least once a term.
PE: Sienna is very passive and completely relies on her friends for motivation. During a hockey or football match, she will literally just jog a few paces behind her best friend the entire time, regardless of where the ball is. She lacks the confidence to make her own tactical decisions. She would improve if she is deliberately placed on a different team to her close friends, forcing her to read the game independently and make her own physical choices.
Sciences: Sienna is very passive and lacks confidence in her own scientific ideas. When asked to write down a hypothesis (a guess about what will happen), she will just lean over and copy whatever her lab partner has written word-for-word. Her target for Year 7 is to write down her own prediction in her book and cover it with her hand before she is allowed to discuss it with her partner.
Humanities: Sienna is very passive in Religious Education. If we are discussing a difficult moral question, she will just wait to hear what her best friend says and then repeat exactly the same opinion. She lacks the confidence to explore her own moral compass. She would improve if she is required to write her own opinion down on a mini-whiteboard and hold it to her chest before anyone in the class is allowed to speak.

Transition Portrait: Eleanor is a genuinely exceptional student with a voracious appetite for learning, reading, and writing. She absorbs complex concepts instantly and frequently reads literature aimed at A-Level students. She is highly mature, preferring the company of adults to her peers, and needs to be placed in top sets immediately to prevent profound boredom.
Home Life & Interests: Eleanor lives with her parents in a highly intellectual household where the arts are heavily celebrated. She is an aspiring playwright; she writes scripts, directs her friends, and creates costumes in her spare time. She regularly attends the Theatre Royal Plymouth and analyzes the performances with the critical eye of an adult.
Maths: Mathematical reasoning is highly sophisticated. Enjoys finding elegant, non-standard solutions to multi-step problems. Can become deeply frustrated when required to show standard KS2 methods for arithmetic she views as rudimentary. Target: Channel her advanced reasoning into writing step-by-step math "guides" or tutorials for her peers.
English: Profoundly gifted. Writes dialogue and play scripts that sound like they were written by an adult. Gets incredibly frustrated and bored by standard KS2 writing prompts, occasionally leading to a condescending attitude toward peers. Target: Provide KS3/KS4 level stimulus materials (like extracts from classic literature or modern plays) for her creative writing.
Creative Arts: Eleanor is incredibly mature and gets genuinely offended when we play basic primary school drama games like "Splat," asking if they can read Shakespeare instead! She draws beautifully but gets impatient with peers who are messy. Her target is to write the script for her group so she feels intellectually challenged, but she must practice letting other people act it out.
PE: Eleanor is profoundly gifted academically and views primary school PE as completely beneath her. She will often sigh loudly, walk instead of run during the warm-up, and complain that chasing a plastic ball is a waste of her brainpower. She would improve if she is given a tactical coaching role, challenging her to analyze the opposing team's formation and devise strategic plays for her peers to execute, engaging her intellect.
Sciences: Eleanor is profoundly gifted and gets genuinely offended when we do standard primary school science like "floating and sinking." She rolls her eyes at baking soda volcanoes and wants to do proper KS3 chemistry. Her target is to be provided with advanced extension questions—like balancing the chemical equation for the reaction she just watched—to keep her intellectually challenged.
Humanities: Eleanor is profoundly gifted and reads adult geopolitical books at home. She views the standard Year 6 curriculum on rivers or the Romans as insulting to her intelligence, often rolling her eyes or correcting the teacher's minor simplifications. She could do better if she is provided with advanced, KS3-level extension questions that ask her to compare historical events to modern-day political situations.

Transition Portrait: Isla is incredibly conscientious and desperate to please her teachers, ensuring her books are impeccably neat and homework is always early. Unfortunately, this conscientiousness manifests as severe anxiety under test conditions, where she will often blank or cry in frustration. She needs constant reassurance that her worth is not tied to a test score.
Home Life & Interests: Isla lives with her mum and three younger siblings in a chaotic but loving home. She naturally takes on a maternal role, helping to get the younger ones ready for school. She is a huge animal lover, spends her allowance on treats for her golden retriever, and hopes to be a vet one day.
Maths: A quiet, careful worker who is highly secure and accurate in her basic KS2 arithmetic operations. Lacks self-belief. Highly reluctant to tackle multi-step problems independently without constant teacher reassurance. Target: Develop independence by attempting at least the first two steps of a reasoning problem before asking for support.
English: Produces beautiful, highly descriptive vocabulary. Her written text analysis is deeply sensitive and perceptive. Far too anxious to read aloud or share her excellent ideas with the whole class during spoken language tasks. Target: Build confidence by sharing one written idea with a trusted, designated "talk partner" before whole-class feedback.
Creative Arts: Isla does lovely, careful coloring and very neat little drawings, keeping her sketchbook perfectly tidy. She is extremely shy, however, and will visibly shake or cry if the teacher asks her to sing or say a line in front of the class. Her target for Year 7 is to always be partnered with her best friend for creative subjects, and to only ever perform in front of the teacher, never the whole class.
PE: Isla is wonderfully attentive and follows all safety instructions perfectly, making her very reliable when getting out heavy gymnastics equipment. She is paralyzingly shy, however, and will actively run away from the ball in team sports so no one looks at her or expects her to score. She would improve if she is partnered with a quiet, supportive peer for racket sports like badminton, building her confidence over a net rather than in a crowded pitch.
Sciences: Isla is a very careful, neat worker who is excellent at following step-by-step instructions safely. She is so quiet, however, that she will let the louder, more confident children in her group take over the entire experiment while she just watches from the side. Her target for Year 7 is to be the only person in her pair allowed to physically handle the equipment, while her louder partner is restricted to just writing down the results.
Humanities: Isla produces the most beautiful, meticulously neat timelines for History, and her written work on the major world religions is deeply respectful and thoughtful. Because she is so terribly shy, she will never put her hand up to share her excellent moral insights during whole-class RE discussions. She could do better in Year 7 if she is encouraged to write her thoughts on a sticky note and put it on the teacher's desk, allowing her voice to be heard anonymously.

Transition Portrait: Daniel is the quintessential "middle of the road" student—perfectly average attainment, perfectly acceptable behavior, and perfectly pleasant to teach. He rarely puts his hand up to answer a question but will usually get it right if called upon. Teachers need to ensure he doesn't slip through the cracks just because he doesn't demand attention.
Home Life & Interests: Daniel lives with his dad and leads a very relaxed, drama-free life outside of school. He is quite sporty and plays squash at a local club, which keeps him fit and teaches him good sportsmanship. He has a tight, stable group of friends whom he has known since nursery.
Maths: The quintessential "expected standard" student. Quietly secure across the entire KS2 curriculum, applying standard methods correctly. Highly passive learner. Will sit quietly if he finishes early rather than asking for extension work or pushing himself. Target: Independently move onto the "Challenge" or Greater Depth questions as soon as the core task is completed.
English: The ultimate dependable student. Spelling, punctuation, and grammar are all solidly at the expected KS2 standard. Very passive. Plays it completely safe with his vocabulary and narrative structures to avoid making any mistakes. Target: Take a creative risk in every piece of extended writing, such as using a flashback or an ambitious new vocabulary word.
Creative Arts: Daniel is a very sweet boy who follows instructions perfectly. He will paint exactly what you tell him to paint, very neatly, and then sit quietly. However, he never takes a creative risk or uses his own imagination. His target is to use a color that isn't realistic—like painting a purple dog or a green sky—just to practice breaking the rules a little bit.
PE: Daniel is a very sweet, compliant boy who always remembers his kit, but he is absolutely terrified of the ball! In any invasion game, he will flinch, close his eyes, and turn his back if a ball is thrown towards him, making him a liability in defense. He could do better if his confidence is built up using large, soft sponge balls in highly controlled pair-drills before he is expected to catch a hard netball or basketball in a match.
Sciences: Daniel is a very sweet boy, but he is terrified of making a mistake. If his experiment turns green, but he knows it was supposed to turn blue, he will actually rub out his own results and copy the "correct" answer from a textbook to play it safe. His target is to write down exactly what actually happens, even if it seems completely wrong.
Humanities: Daniel is a very sweet, safe worker who will happily copy the entire History textbook word-for-word if you let him. He is terrified of giving the "wrong" answer in RE, so he never uses his own words or shares his own thoughts. He would improve if he is given tasks where the textbook is physically taken away, forcing him to summarize a historical event using his own vocabulary.

Transition Portrait: Noah is a highly logical and analytical thinker who excels in subjects with clear rules, like maths and computer science. However, he treats learning like a race and will often rush through his work just to be the first one finished, sacrificing neatness and accuracy. He needs to be challenged with open-ended problems that cannot be solved quickly.
Home Life & Interests: Noah lives with his dad, who works in IT and heavily encourages Noah's interest in technology. Noah attends a local weekend coding club where he is learning Python and basic robotics. He is very close to his dad, and they bond over strategy board games and watching science fiction films.
Maths: Working securely at Greater Depth. Approaches algebra and complex number sequences with exceptional, logical precision. Can occasionally over-rely on his strong mental math for complex equations, leading to minor sign (+/-) errors. Target: Consistently lay out multi-step algebraic equations clearly on paper, line by line, to avoid transcription errors.
English: Strong grasp of complex grammar rules. Highly logical non-fiction writer who structures arguments flawlessly. Finds creative writing completely arbitrary and highly frustrating; struggles to invent narratives or characters from scratch. Target: Use rigid narrative templates and character-building questionnaires to provide a logical framework for creative writing.
Creative Arts: Noah is great at using the computers to make beats on GarageBand, as he treats it like a logical puzzle. He hates drawing, though—if he can't draw a face perfectly the first time, he gets very frustrated and says art is stupid. His target for Year 7 Art is to try doing pixel art, geometric patterns, or graphic design on a computer, which fits his logical brain much better than painting.
PE: Noah approaches sports mathematically; he is brilliant at analyzing the trajectory of a cricket ball or figuring out the best angle for a long jump. He absolutely hates expressive dance or gymnastics, finding the lack of a "winning score" entirely pointless. He could do better if expressive movement is framed as a biomechanical exercise, focusing on strength, balance, and muscle control rather than artistic expression.
Sciences: Noah treats science like a giant math puzzle; he loves looking at a table of results and immediately spotting the numerical pattern. He hates writing long, descriptive conclusions about what he saw, viewing the words as inefficient compared to the numbers. His target is to be allowed to use strict bullet points to structure his conclusions, focusing purely on "evidence" and "reasoning" rather than flowery descriptions.
Humanities: Noah treats Geography like a giant math lesson, which means he is brilliant at analyzing population data, reading climate graphs, and calculating distances on a map! He flatly refuses to write empathetic History tasks, like writing a letter home from the trenches, finding it illogical. He could do better if he is allowed to demonstrate his historical understanding through writing factual newspaper reports rather than pretending to be an emotional character.

Transition Portrait: James is a genuinely sweet boy who tries incredibly hard in lessons but struggles significantly with working memory and retaining information week on week. He will understand a concept perfectly on a Tuesday but look at you blankly when asked to recall it on a Thursday. He relies heavily on knowledge organizers, dual coding, and constant spaced retrieval practice.
Home Life & Interests: James lives with his dad, who is deeply loving but sometimes struggles to understand why James finds school so hard, as "he tries his best." They bond over a shared love of professional wrestling; James knows the stats and backstories of every WWE wrestler and loves talking to staff about recent matches.
Maths: highly enthusiastic and sweet-natured learner who responds excellently to visual math models (like the Bar Model). Working memory struggles mean he frequently forgets times tables and multi-step methods when under assessment pressure. Target: Consistently use a physical multiplication grid or visual reference sheet to reduce cognitive load during reasoning tasks.
English: Very enthusiastic and willing to share his ideas verbally. Enjoys highly visual texts and graphic novels. Working memory struggles mean he frequently forgets spelling rules and the sequence of events in longer novels. Target: Use a physical "story map" or graphic organizer to track plot points and character arcs as he reads through a longer text.
Creative Arts: James is a very sweet boy who gets super excited about making masks or doing big, messy art projects. His working memory is very weak, so he will often forget his lines five seconds after you tell him, or forget which color he was supposed to be using. His target is to always have his script in his hand (even during the final performance) and to have a visual checklist on his desk for art tasks.
PE: James is wonderfully enthusiastic and loves the warm-up games like "bulldog" or "tag"! His working memory is very weak, so in a game like basketball, he will frequently forget which direction his team is shooting, or forget the rule that he has to bounce the ball, ending up running with it. He would improve if he wears a colored bib that matches his team's goalpost, providing a constant visual reminder of which way to run.
Sciences: James is a very enthusiastic boy who loves the messy side of science! His working memory is very weak, so he will frequently forget how many drops of a chemical he just put in a beaker, or he will forget to press start on the stopwatch, ruining the experiment. His target is to always have a physical, step-by-step checklist on the desk, physically ticking off each step the moment he finishes it.
Humanities: James is so enthusiastic and loves looking at old maps and historical photos! His working memory is very weak, so if we learn about the water cycle on Tuesday, by Thursday he will have completely forgotten words like "evaporation" or "precipitation." He could do better by using active recall games, like matching cards or online quizzes, at the start of every single Geography lesson to constantly refresh his memory.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Trauma/Attachment needs. Reacts poorly to shouting; needs de-escalation scripts.
Transition Portrait: Max's early childhood trauma severely impacts his ability to trust adults and regulate his emotions in the classroom. He is hyper-vigilant; a teacher raising their voice to the class can trigger a panic response, leading him to overturn desks or swear. He requires unconditional positive regard, highly consistent boundaries, and scripted de-escalation from familiar staff.
Home Life & Interests: Max was placed into long-term foster care two years ago. His foster parents are incredibly patient and work closely with the school's safeguarding team. Max struggles to form attachments to humans but is deeply bonded to the family's rescue dog, showing intense empathy and care for animals that he cannot yet show to peers.
Maths: Secure in core addition and subtraction when following a highly predictable, repetitive routine. Finds the abstract nature of fractions and missing number problems highly triggering; will often tear up his work in frustration. Target: Use a personalized mini-whiteboard to allow for easily erasable mistakes, reducing the permanence of errors.
English: When he feels emotionally safe, he can write incredibly poignant, empathetic responses to characters facing hardship. The "blank page" is highly triggering. Will often exhibit complete task refusal and defensive posturing during writing assessments. Target: Always provide highly structured sentence starters or a graphic organizer to completely remove the pressure of a blank page.
Creative Arts: Max finds the freedom of art really overwhelming; if you give him a blank piece of paper and say "draw what you want," he will panic and put his head on the desk. He prefers copying step-by-step videos. His target is to always be given a template or a starting shape to trace so he doesn't have to face the pressure of a blank page.
PE: Max is an active refuser. The loud echo of the sports hall, the shrill whistle, and the chaotic changing rooms trigger severe anxiety for him, resulting in him hiding in the toilets or having a meltdown before PE even begins. He could do much better if his transition to secondary PE is heavily heavily adapted, allowing him to wear his own comfortable tracksuit and participate in 1-on-1 sports like badminton in a quiet, separate space until he feels safe.
Sciences: Max finds blank pages highly triggering. If you give him a blank worksheet and ask him to "write a method for filtering muddy water," he will freeze completely and put his head on the desk. His target to build his scientific confidence is to always be given a highly structured, fill-in-the-blanks template for his method and conclusion.
Humanities: Max finds open-ended tasks highly triggering. If you give him a blank page and ask him to "write what you know about the Ancient Egyptians," he will panic and shut down completely. He could do much better if he is always provided with highly structured, fill-in-the-blank templates or explicit sentence starters to guide his writing in Humanities.

Transition Portrait: Rowan is a deeply thoughtful and highly creative student who prefers to observe rather than be the center of attention. They do their best work in quiet, small-group settings where they feel safe expressing their ideas. They can be quite sensitive to criticism, so feedback needs to be framed as constructive and supportive rather than purely corrective.
Home Life & Interests: Rowan lives in a shared housing setup with their mum, and money can sometimes be tight, though mum ensures they never go without essentials. Rowan is an incredible artist who spends hours drawing intricate anime-style characters and writing accompanying backstories. They use their art as a primary coping mechanism for stress.
Maths: Grasps visual elements of math brilliantly, particularly reflecting and translating shapes on a 4-quadrant coordinate grid. Formal algebraic notation and retaining formal written methods for long division require ongoing practice. Target: Practice translating visual sequences and patterns into formal algebraic expressions (e.g., finding the nth term).
English: A stylistically original, highly creative, and visual writer. Uses extended metaphors and vivid imagery brilliantly. Struggles to adapt their highly creative voice to rigid, formal non-fiction formats (e.g., formal letters, newspaper reports). Target: Practice explicitly changing tone and register to suit specific, formal audiences in transactional writing.
Creative Arts: Rowan is super creative and spends hours drawing really quirky, imaginative characters and doodles. They do get a bit stubborn when asked to do standard school tasks, like basic shading or drawing a bowl of fruit, preferring just to draw their own anime-style art. Their target is to try and complete one basic "technical" drawing step—like practicing shading or mixing colors—before they go back to their own creative style.
PE: Rowan is incredibly creative and absolutely shines in expressive dance, constructing beautiful and complex routines. They absolutely despise traditional, muddy outdoor sports like football or rugby, often standing completely still with their arms crossed to avoid getting dirty. They would improve if they are encouraged to apply their excellent footwork and agility from dance to evasion games like dodgeball or tag rugby.
Sciences: Rowan is incredibly artistic and draws the most beautiful, detailed, colorful diagrams of plants and animal cells! They do find the mathematical side of science really boring, though, and will often draw a bar chart without using a ruler or adding any numbers. Their target for Year 7 is to focus purely on the accuracy of their data, ensuring all graphs have straight lines and correct labels before they are allowed to add any color.
Humanities: Rowan is so creative and produces the most breathtaking, artistic title pages and diagrams for our topics on Ancient Egypt and Rainforests! They completely refuse to learn the boring, factual stuff though; they will draw a beautiful river but refuse to label the meander or the oxbow lake. They could do better by ensuring that every piece of beautiful artwork they produce in Geography or History is accurately labeled with at least five factual keywords.

Transition Portrait: Sophie is a perpetually cheerful, sunny presence in the classroom. She approaches every task with enthusiasm, works well with absolutely anyone, and bounces back quickly from setbacks. She is a fantastic collaborative learner and naturally takes on the role of 'encourager' in group projects, making sure everyone feels included and heard.
Home Life & Interests: Sophie lives with her parents and is deeply involved in competitive cheerleading, which requires intense physical fitness, teamwork, and trust. She trains multiple times a week and travels for competitions. The team-oriented nature of her sport translates perfectly into her positive, highly collaborative attitude in the classroom.
Maths: Brings wonderful collaborative energy to group investigations. Secure in formal operations, shape, and angles. Lacks resilience when tackling unfamiliar multi-step problems independently; tends to give up if the first step isn't perfectly obvious. Target: Attempt the first two operational steps of any reasoning problem independently before requesting assistance from a teacher or peer.
English: Highly enthusiastic reader who loves collaborative drama, roleplay, and group brainstorming sessions. Struggles significantly with independent, silent writing stamina; she frequently gets distracted and wants to share her ideas rather than write them down. Target: Build independent writing stamina by completing a strict 15-minute silent drafting phase before any peer collaboration is allowed.
Creative Arts: Sophie is a lovely, enthusiastic cheerleader for her friends, but she treats Drama rehearsals like a giant playdate! She gets the giggles constantly and will break character mid-sentence to wave at her best friend in the audience. Her target for Year 7 is to stay in character for a full, three-minute scene without breaking, laughing, or making eye contact with her friends off-stage.
PE: Sophie is the ultimate cheerleader; she is wonderfully supportive, always clapping for her friends and bringing great positivity to the sports hall! She is so busy watching and cheering for everyone else, however, that she frequently forgets to actually play the game herself, often letting the ball roll right past her feet. She could do better if she is challenged to focus purely on her own physical performance for a full ten minutes before she is allowed to cheer for anyone else.
Sciences: Sophie is so lovely and enthusiastic, but she treats group experiments like a fun crafting session with her friends. She will happily let her experiment boil over or ruin her results because she is too busy trying to make her results table look pretty with three different colored gel pens. Her target is that no colored pens or rulers are allowed on her desk until the entire practical is finished and packed away.
Humanities: Sophie is so lovely and enthusiastic, but she treats Humanities projects like a fun crafting session. She will happily spend forty minutes decorating the title page of her History book with gel pens, leaving only five minutes to actually write the essay. She could do better if she is given a strict five-minute timer for any coloring or decorating, ensuring her focus remains on the written content.

Transition Portrait: Alfie is a highly enthusiastic student who loves to contribute to class discussions, though he often calls out without raising his hand. He works best in highly structured environments with clear, short-term goals. Without a firm seating plan, he is easily distracted by his peers and will try to engage them in off-topic conversations.
Home Life & Interests: Alfie lives with his dad and twin brother, Archie. The household is sports-obsessed, and Alfie is a massive Plymouth Argyle fan, attending home games whenever possible. Dad is very supportive but works long hours, meaning Alfie and his brother often manage their own homework schedules, which can lead to missed deadlines.
Maths: Excellent mental arithmetic and rapid recall of multiplication facts. Very quick to grasp new concepts. Tends to rush multi-step word problems, leading to careless arithmetic errors. Struggles to maintain focus on extended tasks. Target: Systematically use inverse operations to check the final answer of every calculation before moving on.
English: Great imagination; produces lively and engaging ideas during creative writing brainstorming sessions. Easily distracted. Rushes his final drafts, leading to the frequent omission of basic punctuation (capital letters and full stops). Target: Systematically proofread work specifically for end-of-sentence punctuation before handing it in.
Creative Arts: Alfie brings a ton of bouncy energy to drama games and loves doing silly voices! However, he is very impulsive and tends to rush his art projects, often finishing a painting in five minutes and then getting distracted. To help him slow down in Year 7, his target for Art is to spend just two quiet minutes planning his drawing or picking his colors before he's allowed to start.
PE: Alfie has boundless energy and incredible natural speed, making him a real asset in athletics and cross-country! However, in invasion games like tag rugby or basketball, he just puts his head down and runs blindly into the opposition, completely forgetting his teammates exist. He would improve if he practiced looking up and actively passing the ball at least three times before he is allowed to attempt a shot on goal.
Sciences: Alfie absolutely loves hands-on science! Give him some baking soda and vinegar and he is thrilled. However, he is incredibly impulsive and will just start pouring liquids together before he has even read the instructions or put his safety goggles on. To keep him safe in a real lab, his Year 7 target is to write down a clear prediction and read the method twice before he is allowed to touch any equipment.
Humanities: Alfie absolutely loves the gory and explosive parts of Humanities! Give him a Geography lesson on volcanoes or a History lesson on Roman gladiators, and he is hooked. He is very impulsive, though, and will try to build a papier-mâché volcano before he has even read what a tectonic plate is. He could do much better in Year 7 by writing down a step-by-step physical process before he is allowed to do any practical modeling.

Transition Portrait: Archie is an intense and driven learner who puts a lot of pressure on himself to succeed, particularly when comparing himself to his brother. He can become deeply frustrated if he doesn't understand a concept immediately. He works far better when seated away from Alfie, allowing him to focus on his own progress rather than competing.
Home Life & Interests: Like his twin, Archie lives with his dad and is a devoted Plymouth Argyle supporter. He plays Sunday league football and is highly competitive on the pitch. His father has noted that Archie often brings his sporting frustrations home, and pastoral staff may need to help him develop better emotional regulation when he feels he is "losing" at a task.
Maths: Working at Greater Depth. Highly accurate with fractions, decimals, and percentages (FDP). Highly driven. His highly competitive nature means he often performs calculations in his head and skips writing down his method to finish "first." Target: Document all working out clearly and systematically to secure essential method marks in KS3 assessments.
English: Reads above age-related expectations. Uses high-level vocabulary and excels in persuasive writing tasks. His competitive nature means he rushes extended writing to be the first one "finished," sacrificing narrative depth and structural pacing. Target: Use a structured "story mountain" or mind map to plan out the pacing of a narrative before beginning to write.
Creative Arts: Archie is a very confident boy who easily memorizes his lines for school plays and is quick to pick up keyboard tunes. Because he's so competitive, he can get a bit bossy during group work, trying to direct his friends rather than working together. His target for Year 7 Drama is to let someone else in his group choose the scene idea and practice being a supportive team player.
PE: Archie is highly skilled, particularly in football, and possesses great stamina and coordination. Unfortunately, his extreme competitiveness means he is a terrible team player; he will openly shout at his classmates if they miss a pass or make a mistake, leading to arguments on the pitch. He could do much better in Year 7 if he is explicitly taught sportsmanship, perhaps by being tasked with giving two genuine compliments to his teammates during every match.
Sciences: Archie has a fantastic memory for science facts and can name all the planets and human organs effortlessly. Because he is so competitive, he treats experiments like a race, rushing to be the first one to pack away, which means his results are often messy or inaccurate. His target is to practice taking three separate readings for an experiment and calculating an average, forcing him to slow down and value accuracy over speed.
Humanities: Archie has a phenomenal memory for historical dates and can recount the entire timeline of WWII flawlessly. However, in Religious Education, his competitive nature means he treats classroom debates on ethics or beliefs as an argument he must "win," often speaking over peers who have different views. He would do better if he practiced active listening, repeating a classmate's religious or moral viewpoint respectfully before offering his own opinion.

Transition Portrait: Gracie is a very consistent, hardworking student who takes pride in her presentation and always tries her best. She is eager to please the teacher and will often volunteer to hand out books or run errands. She can occasionally be a little too eager to report the minor misbehavior of her peers, so she needs guidance on focusing on her own learning.
Home Life & Interests: Gracie lives with her parents and is incredibly passionate about nature and the environment. She has her own section of the family garden where she grows vegetables and wildflowers from seed. She is highly responsible, caring, and often brings in produce she has grown to give to her favorite teachers.
Maths: Applies incredible diligence to her work. Highly secure in data handling, statistics, and creating charts (often linking to environmental data). Needs to build confidence in abstract algebra; can become hesitant and anxious when numbers are entirely replaced by letters. Target: Practice substituting values into simple algebraic expressions related to real-world contexts (e.g., calculating carbon footprints).
English: Incredibly diligent. Writes passionate, highly researched persuasive texts and articles, particularly on environmental issues. Tends to only read non-fiction. Can struggle to identify subtle character motivations or unreliable narrators in complex fiction texts. Target: Track a single character's emotional journey throughout a novel, quoting specific evidence to show how their feelings change over time.
Creative Arts: Gracie is highly diligent, but she is a bit of a one-trick pony! Every single piece of artwork she does is about saving the turtles, and every play she writes is about recycling. It's lovely, but she needs to stretch her imagination. Her target is to create a piece of art or a short scene that has absolutely nothing to do with the real world or the environment, purely for the sake of having fun.
PE: Gracie is highly diligent and always tries her best in athletics. However, during outdoor sports like cross-country or orienteering, she will frequently stop the entire group to lecture them about not stepping on the wildflowers or disturbing the local bugs, completely abandoning the race! She would improve if she is reminded to keep her focus strictly on the physical objective during the lesson, saving her environmental surveys for science time.
Sciences: Gracie is highly diligent, but she tries to turn every single science topic into a lesson on climate change or saving the ocean—even when we are just learning about simple circuits or magnets! It's lovely, but she misses the actual learning objective. Her target is to write a conclusion that focuses strictly on the specific physics or chemistry of the lesson, without mentioning plastic pollution once.
Humanities: Gracie is highly diligent, but she tries to turn every single Geography and History topic into a lesson on climate change or saving the ocean—even when we are learning about the Romans! It's lovely, but she misses the actual learning objective. She would improve if she is gently redirected to focus strictly on the specific social or political history of the lesson, without forcing an eco-angle.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed ASD with PDA traits. Requires indirect phrasing rather than direct demands.
Transition Portrait: Harrison's profile is complex; his Pathological Demand Avoidance means that direct instructions (e.g., "Harrison, sit down and write this") cause an immediate spike in anxiety and outright refusal. He is highly intelligent but must be given perceived autonomy (e.g., "I wonder if we should start with question 1 or 2 today?"). He is a highly visual learner.
Home Life & Interests: Harrison's parents are exceptional advocates for him and utilize low-demand parenting strategies at home, which they heavily encourage the school to mirror. His special interest is architecture, specifically recreating famous buildings using Lego. He can spend six straight hours focused on a build, demonstrating incredible attention to detail.
Maths: Highly capable in logical reasoning and spatial puzzles. Often sees alternative ways to solve a problem. PDA profile means he will completely shut down and refuse to work if tasks feel overly prescriptive or forced by the teacher. Target: Provide autonomy by offering a choice of tasks (e.g., "Choose any 5 of these 10 equations to solve").
English: Highly articulate and capable of deep, philosophical insights into character motives when he chooses to engage. His PDA profile means he will flatly refuse to write if given a highly prescriptive, restrictive essay prompt. Target: Provide two or three essay prompt options to give him a sense of autonomy and control over his written output.
Creative Arts: Harrison is very capable, but his PDA means he point-blank refuses to join in if he feels forced. If you tell him he must sing or must draw a specific flower, he will just put his head on the desk and ignore you. His target for Year 7 is to always be given a choice of three different tasks (e.g., "You can use clay, paint, or pastels today") so he feels in control of his own creativity.
PE: Harrison is a very capable athlete, but his PDA profile makes him a frequent refuser in PE. If the teacher blows a whistle and demands everyone line up for a specific passing drill, he will simply sit down on the grass and refuse to move, hating the lack of autonomy. He could do much better if he is given a choice of two warm-up activities (e.g., "Do you want to practice your dribbling or your shooting today?"), giving him the control he needs to participate.
Sciences: Harrison is very capable, but his PDA profile means he hates being micro-managed. If a teacher gives him a rigid, step-by-step instruction sheet and tells him he must follow it exactly, he will push the equipment away and refuse to participate. His target is to be given a sense of autonomy by choosing his own equipment (e.g., "Do you want to use the 100ml beaker or the 250ml beaker for this?").
Humanities: Harrison has a brilliant mind for Geography and understands complex concepts like the water cycle easily. However, his PDA profile means he hates being told exactly what to research; if given a rigid worksheet on a specific country, he will flatly refuse to do it. He would improve if the teacher provides broad project briefs (e.g., "Research any natural disaster of your choice") to give him the autonomy he needs to engage.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed ASD. Heavily masks in class. Needs clear, literal instructions.
Transition Portrait: Mia is highly adept at 'masking' her autism in the classroom, meaning she mimics the behavior of neurotypical girls to fit in. This requires immense cognitive effort and leaves her completely exhausted by 3 PM. She hates vague instructions (e.g., "Write a bit about this") and needs exact parameters (e.g., "Write three bullet points summarizing the text").
Home Life & Interests: Mia lives with her mum, who reports that Mia frequently experiences "meltdowns" at home as a release from the pressure of masking all day at school. Her special interest is painting incredibly detailed fantasy miniatures. This highly focused, solitary hobby acts as a regulatory tool to help her decompress after a noisy school day.
Maths: Works exhaustingly hard to mask her struggles. Memorizes calculation methods by rote to keep up with peers. Lacks the conceptual understanding to apply rote-learned methods to novel or flipped reasoning contexts. Target: Use concrete manipulatives (like Dienes blocks or Cuisenaire rods) to build a visual understanding of why a method works.
English: Copies writing models and structures perfectly. Produces incredibly neat, highly presented work to blend in. Her heavy masking means she frequently nods and agrees during text analysis without actually comprehending the underlying inference. Target: Check for genuine understanding by asking her to explain a concept in her own words, rather than just repeating the teacher's explanation.
Creative Arts: Mia works very hard to blend in. In Art, she will wait to see what the girl next to her is drawing, and then draw exactly the same thing. In choir, she just mouths the words rather than singing out loud. Her target is to sit at a desk by herself for the first five minutes of Art to come up with her own original idea before she is allowed to sit with her friends.
PE: Mia works very hard to blend in during PE. If the class is doing an aerobics routine, she will wait a split second to see what the girl next to her does, and then copy the movement exactly so she doesn't stand out or make a mistake. She could do better if she is gently pushed to lead a simple, three-step warm-up for a small group, forcing her to trust her own physical memory rather than copying others.
Sciences: Mia works very hard to mask her confusion by blending in. During an experiment, she will hover and wait to see what color the girl next to her writes down, and then she will write down exactly the same thing to avoid standing out. Her target is to use a physical screen (like a folder) around her desk during independent observations so she has to trust her own eyes.
Humanities: Mia works very hard to blend in. During Geography, she will hover and wait to see what the girl next to her is coloring on her map, and then she will color exactly the same thing to avoid standing out. She could do better if she is given independent, randomized mini-projects (e.g., everyone on the table gets a different country to map) so she has to trust her own geographical skills.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Hemiplegic CP. Uses a laptop for extended typing.
Transition Portrait: Thomas is a fantastic self-advocate who manages his physical limitations with maturity and good humor. His cognitive ability is right on average, but he experiences severe fatigue in his hand and arm during long writing tasks, so his laptop is essential. He works well with everyone and is particularly keen to contribute to verbal class debates.
Home Life & Interests: Thomas lives with his parents, who have instilled in him a "no excuses" mindset while remaining fiercely protective of his rights. He is a massive sports fan, particularly rugby, and while he cannot play contact sports, he manages the statistics for a local youth team. He also enjoys complex strategy board games and historical documentaries.
Maths: Verbally articulates his reasoning brilliantly. Uses physical manipulatives exceptionally well to calculate volume and surface area. Mild cerebral palsy means his written recording speed can be slow, sometimes causing frustration during timed tasks. Target: Effectively use assistive tech (e.g., a laptop or scribe) to capture his excellent verbal reasoning during extended math investigations.
English: Phenomenal verbal communicator. His spoken analysis of literature and character motives is highly perceptive. Cerebral palsy makes extended handwriting physically exhausting, slow, and sometimes painful. Target: Consistently use a laptop or speech-to-text software for all extended writing tasks to allow his written output to match his verbal brilliance.
Creative Arts: Thomas comes up with the funniest, most brilliant ideas for plays and is great at directing his friends verbally! Because of his cerebral palsy, physically holding a paintbrush for a long time or trying to play a recorder is really tiring and frustrating for him. His target to make sure his creativity isn't blocked is to always be allowed to use an iPad for digital drawing or a switch-accessible keyboard for music.
PE: Thomas has a brilliant strategic mind and is an excellent team captain for target games like boccia or indoor curling! Because his cerebral palsy severely limits his mobility and stamina, he gets exhausted very quickly and can be knocked over in chaotic sports like tag rugby. He would improve if his peers are explicitly taught how to play inclusive, seated versions of sports (like sitting volleyball) so they can compete on a level playing field.
Sciences: Thomas has a fantastic scientific brain and is brilliant at verbally explaining why a reaction happened or how a circuit works! Because of his cerebral palsy, physically pouring liquids, connecting fiddly wires, or lighting a match is very difficult and tiring. His target is to act as the "Chief Scientist" during group work, verbally directing a partner to do the physical setup while he records the data on a tablet.
Humanities: Thomas has an encyclopedic knowledge of WWII and can verbally explain the causes of the war with the vocabulary of a teenager! Because his cerebral palsy makes handwriting so slow and tiring, he will often just write three words to get the task over with, which doesn't reflect his brilliant brain. He could do better if he always has access to a laptop or a scribe for all extended History and RE essays to unlock his full potential.

Transition Portrait: Ethan is a mathematically minded student who enjoys problem-solving and logical puzzles. He has a great sense of humor but is highly susceptible to peer influence; if he sits near the 'class clowns', he will join in, but if seated near focused students, he will work diligently. Strategic seating plans are absolutely crucial for Ethan's success.
Home Life & Interests: Ethan lives with his parents and is a very active, sporty child. He plays tennis at a high club level, training multiple evenings a week, which requires a lot of discipline. His parents hold him to high behavioral standards and are fully supportive of any sanctions the school issues if he misbehaves.
Maths: Has a solid foundation in number fluency and times tables. Quick to grasp new concepts when focused. Highly distracted by peers. Will frequently stop working mid-calculation to chat, leading to incomplete tasks. Target: Complete a clearly defined, non-negotiable chunk of independent work before engaging in any peer collaboration.
English: Confident speaking aloud and participating in class reading; uses a highly expressive and engaging tone. Highly distracted during independent writing blocks; written work is frequently unfinished due to chatting with peers. Target: Complete a 15-minute silent, independent writing block before being allowed to share work or discuss ideas collaboratively.
Creative Arts: Ethan is incredibly chatty and confident, which makes him great at projecting his voice in a play. The downside is that he physically cannot stop talking, which means he talks over the teacher's instructions in Music and distracts everyone at his table in Art. His target is to complete ten full minutes of absolute, silent drawing or focused listening before he's allowed to whisper to his partner.
PE: Ethan is so incredibly chatty that he treats PE like a social event! He will literally stop running in the middle of a football match to chat with the opposing team, completely forgetting to mark his player and letting them score. He could do better if he is given specific, competitive marking assignments (e.g., "Do not let the player in the blue bib get the ball"), channeling his focus onto a single, immediate task rather than socializing.
Sciences: Ethan is so chatty that he frequently talks all the way through the teacher's safety demonstration. This means that when he gets his equipment, he has no idea what he is actually supposed to be doing and will just start mixing things randomly. His target is that he must verbally repeat the core safety instruction back to the teacher before he is allowed to collect his equipment tray.
Humanities: Ethan is so incredibly chatty that he treats RE debates like a casual chat with his mates, frequently talking over people and making inappropriate jokes. When given a historical text to read, he will talk through the entire reading time and learn nothing. He could do better by completing a strict five-minute silent reading and highlighting phase of any historical source before he is allowed to open his mouth and discuss it.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Level 1 ASD. Uses ear defenders. Concrete thinker.
Transition Portrait: George is highly intelligent, with a phenomenal memory for facts and figures, but he finds the social environment of the classroom exhausting. He interprets language very literally and will point out logical flaws in a teacher's instructions. Group work is a major trigger for him, and he requires explicit permission to work independently when he is overwhelmed.
Home Life & Interests: George lives with his parents, who have structured their home environment to be as calming and predictable as possible. He has a profound, encyclopedic knowledge of the UK train network and spends his weekends trainspotting or playing transport simulation games. His parents are very proactive in communicating his sensory needs to the school.
Maths: Exceptional at calculating complex time intervals, interpreting timetables, and reading statistical graphs. Struggles significantly with the abstract language and idioms often used in complex reasoning word problems. Target: Practice translating abstract word problems into concrete visual models (such as the Bar Model) to find the operation.
English: Exceptional at literal comprehension and rapidly retrieving specific information and facts from non-fiction texts. Struggles significantly to infer abstract meaning, subtext, or understand complex character emotions in fiction. Target: Use visual "emotion charts" to help decode and map how a character is feeling based on their actions in the text.
Creative Arts: George loves drawing highly detailed pictures of trains, maps, and buildings, and he is very good at it. Because of his autism, he finds "pretend" roleplay or acting out abstract emotions in Drama really confusing and upsetting. A great target for him in Year 7 is to let him be the "stage manager" or handle the lighting/sound on the iPad during group plays so he is involved without having to act.
PE: George absolutely loves athletics because it is measurable and logical; he enjoys timing his 100m sprints and trying to beat his personal best. Because of his autism, the chaotic, unpredictable nature of team sports like football causes him immense stress, especially if the referee makes a subjective call he disagrees with. He would improve if he is allowed to focus heavily on individual sports (like swimming, badminton, or track) where the rules are absolute.
Sciences: George is exceptional at classifying materials and loves learning highly specific facts about the periodic table and dinosaurs. Because of his autism, he is very rigid; if an experiment in the classroom doesn't match the textbook exactly, he becomes very distressed and confused. His Year 7 target is to practice writing a conclusion that specifically explains the difference between a "perfect" textbook theory and a "messy" real-world classroom result.
Humanities: George thrives on the absolute, literal facts of Geography; he can name every capital city in Europe and loves drawing flags. Because of his autism, he finds Religious Education deeply frustrating, constantly asking why we are learning about beliefs or miracles that cannot be scientifically proven. He could do better approaching RE through a sociological lens—studying what religious people actually do (festivals, food, charity) rather than getting stuck on abstract faith.

Transition Portrait: Zara is an exceptionally talented mathematician who can solve complex algebraic equations in her head. However, she is painfully shy in humanities subjects and will blush deeply if asked to read aloud in English. She needs teachers to pre-warn her if they are going to ask her a question, giving her time to formulate her answer without panic.
Home Life & Interests: Zara lives with her parents and older siblings, all of whom are high academic achievers. The family is very close-knit and frequently plays competitive sports together on weekends. Zara is a talented badminton player and competes in regional tournaments, though she is very modest about her success.
Maths: Profoundly gifted. Calculates complex FDP (fractions, decimals, percentages) problems flawlessly in her head. Extremely timid. Will never volunteer an answer verbally or share her brilliant methods with the rest of the class. Target: Build confidence by writing her advanced solutions on the main whiteboard before the rest of the class enters the room.
English: Impeccable grasp of complex grammar rules. Her written reading comprehension is highly accurate and detailed. Her extreme shyness means she is terrified of reading aloud or participating in spoken language or drama assessments. Target: Assess her speaking and listening skills through highly structured, quiet 1-on-1 conversations rather than whole-class presentations.
Creative Arts: Zara actually has a beautiful singing voice, but if she notices anyone looking at her, she will instantly stop and stare at the floor. She also draws incredibly detailed anime characters but hides them in her tray. Her target is to record herself singing or playing an instrument on an iPad in a separate room so she can be graded without the panic of an audience.
PE: Zara is surprisingly fast and has great natural agility! However, her extreme shyness means she is absolutely terrified of being watched; she will refuse to take a penalty kick or participate in a gymnastics showcase because she hates having the class's eyes on her. She would improve if all of her assessments and matches are conducted in small, private groups of four, completely removing the pressure of a whole-class audience.
Sciences: Zara is incredibly observant, careful, and will complete an entire scientific investigation flawlessly. However, she is so paralyzingly shy that she will never volunteer her results during a whole-class discussion, even when she is the only one who got it right. Her target is to share her written results table with the teacher at the end of the lesson to ensure her hard work is recognized.
Humanities: Zara has impeccable grammar and her written essays on the social impact of the Industrial Revolution are stunningly mature. She is incredibly shy, however, and will visibly shake if asked to read her work aloud or participate in a verbal RE debate. She would improve if her participation in debates is managed through a written "live-chat" on the classroom whiteboard, allowing her to argue her points silently.

Transition Portrait: Benjamin is the definition of a dependable, low-maintenance student. He listens carefully, follows instructions precisely, and produces neat, accurate work. He excels in independent tasks where he can put his head down and concentrate, though he can be a little rigid in his thinking and sometimes struggles with creative, abstract tasks.
Home Life & Interests: Benjamin lives with his parents and is a serious outdoor enthusiast. His main passion is indoor rock climbing and bouldering, which he does several times a week. This sport requires intense physical problem-solving and risk assessment, traits that perfectly mirror his methodical, careful approach to his schoolwork.
Maths: A highly grounded student who approaches complex reasoning methodically. Understanding of volume, area, and perimeter is particularly strong. Needs to build speed and fluency when converting between different metric units of measurement (e.g., mm to m, g to kg). Target: Tackle multi-stage reasoning problems that specifically require unit conversions before the final calculation can be made.
English: Very solid literal comprehension skills and writes clear, highly logical, and accurate instructional texts. Struggles significantly to grasp abstract themes in poetry or classic literature; tends to take metaphors far too literally. Target: Use dual-coding (drawing a quick visual sketch next to a quote) to help decode and conceptualize complex poetic metaphors.
Creative Arts: Benjamin is a very literal, grounded boy. If you ask him to act like a tree, he will just stand up straight and not move a muscle. He finds abstract art really confusing and prefers building strong, sturdy things out of cardboard. His target for Year 7 is to practice exaggerated mimes (like pretending to pull a giant, heavy rope) to get him used to moving his body expressively.
PE: Benjamin is incredibly strong, very sturdy, and an absolute powerhouse in the scrum during tag rugby! He lacks agility, however, and struggles significantly with sports that require fast footwork, twisting, or balance, like tennis or gymnastics. He could do better if his warm-ups focus heavily on agility ladder drills and fast-paced directional changes to loosen up his rigid movement style.
Sciences: Benjamin is very grounded and literal. He is brilliant at building strong structures (like bridges out of dry spaghetti or paper), but he gets very confused by abstract concepts he can't actually see, like "energy," "particles," or "forces." His target is to use physical, visual models (like drawing arrows on the desk with a whiteboard pen, or using marbles) to help him understand invisible forces.
Humanities: Benjamin is very grounded and loves the physical, measurable side of Geography, like learning how rivers erode rock. He struggles significantly with RE because he takes religious parables and metaphors completely literally, getting confused as to why someone would build a house on sand. He could do better if religious parables are explicitly framed as "fables with a hidden meaning" before he begins to read them.

Transition Portrait: Imogen is a highly social, bubbly girl who sees school primarily as an opportunity to catch up with her friends. She is intelligent enough to scrape by without putting in maximum effort, but she is heavily distracted by classroom gossip. She requires strict seating plans, frequent redirection, and tasks that are broken down into short, highly accountable chunks.
Home Life & Interests: Imogen lives with her mum and is highly focused on aesthetics, fashion, and makeup. She frequently attempts to push the boundaries of the school uniform policy (e.g., rolling up her skirt or wearing subtle makeup) and is deeply invested in social media trends. Her mum tries to monitor this but admits Imogen is very headstrong.
Maths: Highly capable of securing Greater Depth. Quick verbal reasoning and a strong grasp of data handling and statistics. Frequently refuses to show working out; prefers to argue with the teacher or chat with peers rather than follow standard methods. Target: Complete a non-negotiable set of independent calculation questions, showing full methods, before being allowed to participate in group tasks.
English: A brilliant, highly confident speaker. Dominates drama tasks and writes highly persuasive, emotive arguments. Completely ignores formal SPaG rules. Written work frequently reads like a stream-of-consciousness text message to her friends. Target: Explicitly practice code-switching between an "informal/social" register and a strict "formal/academic" register before drafting.
Creative Arts: Imogen is incredibly loud, wildly confident, and loves being the center of attention! The problem is she hates taking direction; if the teacher asks her to sing a bit quieter or change her character's voice, she will argue back and defend her "artistic vision." Her target is to successfully follow a piece of stage direction from a peer or teacher without arguing or rolling her eyes.
PE: Imogen is incredibly confident, very fast, and an excellent netballer! The problem is she hates taking direction from the referee; if the teacher blows the whistle on her for a foul, she will argue back, roll her eyes, and boldly defend her actions on the pitch. She would improve if she is explicitly taught the core value of sportsmanship, practicing accepting a referee's decision immediately and silently, without any backchat.
Sciences: Imogen treats the science lab like her own personal stage! She is so busy being dramatic, posing with the equipment, and entertaining her table that she will completely ignore the teacher's safety warnings. Because lab safety is non-negotiable, her target is that she must correctly explain the main safety hazard of the lesson to the teacher before she is allowed to put her goggles on.
Humanities: Imogen is incredibly confident and loves dominating debates about ethical issues! The problem is she treats all her written Humanities work like a text message to her friends, completely ignoring formal structure and using informal slang. She could do better by explicitly practicing how to write formal newspaper reports, forcing her to adopt an academic, objective register.

Transition Portrait: Lily is an exceptionally gifted student, particularly in mathematics, where she frequently completes extension work ahead of her peers. She is articulate, confident, and very comfortable conversing with adults. She occasionally becomes impatient with classmates who do not grasp concepts as quickly as she does, so she requires guidance on how to be a supportive peer mentor.
Home Life & Interests: Both of Lily's parents are secondary school teachers, and education is highly valued in their household. She is heavily involved in extracurricular activities, most notably playing the violin at a Grade 5 standard. She practices daily and performs with a local youth orchestra, making her very disciplined but sometimes exhausted by her schedule.
Maths: Produces flawless arithmetic and has fully mastered the KS2 curriculum. Impeccable presentation of work. Becomes highly anxious and rigid when faced with open-ended reasoning investigations without an obvious "right" path. Target: Practice estimation techniques to build confidence in taking educated guesses when the final answer isn't immediately clear.
English: Immaculate presentation. Flawless SPaG (Spelling, Punctuation, and Grammar). Reads significantly above age level. Perfectionism completely blocks her creative risk-taking. She will sit staring at a blank page out of fear of making a mistake. Target: Participate in "rapid drafting" exercises where using a pen (and crossing out mistakes rather than erasing) is mandatory.
