Creative Arts: Lily's artwork is incredibly neat, and she practices her keyboard tunes until she gets every single note right. But she is such a perfectionist that she will often rub her drawings out until there's a hole in the paper if she thinks she made a mistake. Her target to help her loosen up is to do a "messy" painting or a drawing with a pen, where she isn't allowed to use an eraser at all.
PE: Lily's gymnastic routines are absolute perfection; she points her toes, remembers every step, and shows incredible balance. However, the unpredictable nature of team sports terrifies her; if a ball is thrown at her in dodgeball or netball, she will duck and cover her head. She would improve if she is slowly introduced to ball sports through highly structured, predictable passing drills in pairs to build her hand-eye coordination and confidence.
Sciences: Lily's science book is immaculate. Her results tables are drawn flawlessly with a ruler and she follows the instructions perfectly. She is so terrified of failing, however, that if her experiment doesn't give the "correct" result, she will panic and try to copy someone else's data. Her target is to learn that an unexpected result isn't a failure, practicing how to write an evaluation about why an experiment might have gone wrong.
Humanities: Lily's Geography map-coloring is perfection; she stays perfectly inside the lines and uses a ruler for every key. However, History gives her terrible anxiety because she is terrified of getting the "wrong" answer when interpreting a primary source like an old photograph. She could do better by learning that history is often about interpretation, practicing answering questions that start with "Why do you think..." where there is no strictly wrong answer.

Transition Portrait: Matilda is a solid, dependable worker who coasted comfortably through primary school without ever really breaking a sweat. Because she rarely struggles, she lacks resilience when finally faced with a task she cannot do immediately. She needs teachers to set high expectations and gently push her out of her comfort zone to reach her true potential.
Home Life & Interests: Matilda lives a very active, outdoorsy life with her parents on the outskirts of the city. Her absolute passion is horse riding; she spends almost all her free time at the local stables, mucking out and riding. This hobby makes her incredibly practical and responsible, though she often arrives at school smelling faintly of hay.
Maths: Highly practical. Confidently applies math to real-world scenarios (money, measures, and simple fractions). Tends to coast. Is entirely secure in the expected standard but rarely pushes herself to tackle Greater Depth challenges. Target: Actively choose the "Extension" or Greater Depth ratio and proportion tasks to stretch her problem-solving skills.
English: Excellent at writing clear, sequential instructions and highly practical non-fiction (e.g., reports, recipes). Reluctant to read fiction; struggles to engage with or analyze abstract metaphors or poetic devices. Target: Read one chapter of a fiction book weekly and identify one simile or metaphor to discuss with a teacher.
Creative Arts: Matilda is incredibly sensible and loves practical tasks like building props. However, she rolls her eyes at anything "expressive" or abstract, preferring her art to look exactly like real life. Her target for Year 7 Drama is to throw herself into one completely silly, over-the-top improvisation game without worrying about looking cool in front of her friends.
PE: Matilda is extremely sensible, grounded, and plays a brilliant game of defense in netball and basketball. She hates anything that requires artistic expression, though, and will look completely miserable and stiff during dance or gymnastics. She would improve if she is allowed to choreograph routines based on martial arts or combat movements (like boxing footwork), leaning into her powerful, grounded style rather than trying to be delicate.
Sciences: Matilda is incredibly practical and loves actually doing the experiments, especially if she gets to build something. However, the minute the practical is over, she refuses to do the written conclusion, claiming it's "boring." Her target for Year 7 is to write just three bullet points explaining why her practical worked, linking her hands-on skills to the scientific theory.
Humanities: Matilda is extremely sensible and grounded in reality. She excels at physical Geography and map reading, but she finds RE completely pointless, often sighing loudly when asked to consider abstract philosophical or religious questions. She could do better by grounding RE in practical reality, focusing heavily on the charitable actions of religious groups (like food banks or community work) rather than abstract faith.

Transition Portrait: Leo is a bright boy whose progress is heavily hindered by a profound lack of interest in the traditional school curriculum. If he cannot see the practical, real-world application of a subject, he simply will not engage. Teachers have the most success with Leo when they contextualize learning (e.g., teaching math through engineering or finance scenarios).
Home Life & Interests: Leo lives with his nan, who struggles to motivate him academically. His sole ambition is to leave school and become a mechanic, and he spends his weekends helping out informally at a local garage run by a family friend. He is incredibly knowledgeable about cars and engines, and praising this knowledge helps build rapport with him.
Maths: Has a strong, intuitive understanding of geometry, properties of shapes, and calculating missing angles. Growing behavioral disengagement. Frequently rushes his arithmetic, guessing answers just to finish the worksheet quickly. Target: Prioritize accuracy over speed; must show the correct written method for at least 5 calculations before moving on.
English: Highly engaged and focused when reading non-fiction texts related to his specific interests (cars, engineering, mechanics). Very little stamina for extended writing; frequently rushes or outright refuses to write more than a single paragraph. Target: Use a bullet-point plan to structure his thoughts before writing, aiming for a minimum of three developed paragraphs per task.
Creative Arts: Leo is obsessed with cars and engineering, meaning every single art project he does somehow turns into a drawing of a sports car! He really dislikes singing or acting and will usually try to sneak to the back of the room to chat. His target is to spend an entire art lesson drawing something that does not have wheels, and to stay on stage for a full scene in Drama.
PE: Leo only cares about how things work mechanically, which means he is great at setting up the trampolines or fixing the badminton nets! During actual team sports, he is completely disengaged, usually volunteering to stand in goal so he can lean against the post and daydream. He could do better if he is rotated out of goal every five minutes, forcing him to play as a midfielder where he actually has to run and engage with the game.
Sciences: Leo is obsessed with engineering, which means he absolutely shines during Physics topics like forces, levers, or electricity! He finds Biology and Chemistry completely pointless and will just put his head on the desk during those lessons. His target is to find the "mechanics" in other sciences—for example, treating the human heart like a car engine to keep him engaged.
Humanities: Leo only cares about how things work. If a History topic involves cars, trains, or building bridges (like the Industrial Revolution), he is fully engaged! If a topic is about politics or religion, he will put his head down and refuse to write. He could do better by finding the "mechanical" angle in any Humanities topic—for example, studying the architecture of a mosque or the logistics of Roman road building to keep him hooked.

Transition Portrait: Millie is a student who always tries her absolute best, but she has a habit of overcomplicating very simple tasks. If asked to write a paragraph, she will attempt to write three pages, often losing the core point of the exercise in the process. She needs teachers to set strict parameters (e.g., "Write exactly four sentences") to help her be concise.
Home Life & Interests: Millie lives with her mum and is heavily influenced by influencer culture. She spends a lot of time filming, editing, and uploading lifestyle vlogs to a private YouTube channel for her friends. While she loves the creative editing process, her mum worries that she is too focused on digital validation.
Maths: Has a solid grasp of percentages, decimals, and money. Thrives during interactive group-work tasks. Treats math lessons as a social event. Needs to focus on rigorous independent practice rather than chatting with peers. Target: Complete a minimum of 15 minutes of silent, independent arithmetic practice before engaging in group discussions.
English: Has a fantastic understanding of "audience" and writes highly engaging, conversational blogs and articles. Frequently slips into informal slang and "text-speak." Treats independent writing time as an opportunity to socialize. Target: Complete a strictly timed 15-minute silent writing block to build stamina before any collaborative group work is permitted.
Creative Arts: Millie treats Drama entirely as a chance to gossip with her friends in the corner, and in Art, she spends twenty minutes drawing bubble-writing of her own name instead of the actual task! Her target is to be partnered with someone who isn't in her immediate friendship group so she actually focuses on the creative task rather than chatting.
PE: Millie views PE entirely as a chance to catch up on playground gossip! She is quite coordinated when she tries, but she will frequently stop in the middle of a tennis rally or a rounders match to chat with the girl next to her, completely missing the ball. She would improve if she is placed in fast-paced, continuous rotational games where the ball is always moving, preventing her from having the time to stand around and socialize.
Sciences: Millie treats science practicals like a coffee morning! She gets so distracted chatting with her friends that she frequently forgets which chemical she has just poured into her beaker, completely ruining the experiment. Her target for Year 7 is to complete a strict, five-minute silent measuring phase before any group discussion is allowed.
Humanities: Millie treats Humanities lessons like a social club! During quiet moments of reflection in RE, she will immediately start whispering to her friends, and her Geography homework is usually rushed so she can get back to chatting. She could do better if silent reflection time is replaced with highly structured "pair-share" tasks, allowing her to channel her need to socialize into the actual topic.

SEND Detail: Suspected ADD. Zones out frequently, loses equipment, struggles to initiate tasks.
Transition Portrait: Oscar is a pleasant boy but suffers from chronic apathy towards his education, frequently arriving without a pen or his planner. He will happily sit in silence staring out the window for an entire lesson if not directly prompted to start writing. He requires intensive scaffolding, task checklists, and immediate praise for task completion.
Home Life & Interests: Oscar lives with his dad, who works night shifts, meaning Oscar has very little supervision in the evenings. Consequently, he spends until the early hours of the morning playing Xbox, leading to severe fatigue during the school day. Pastoral intervention has been attempted to help establish a healthier sleep routine, with limited success.
Maths: Grasps concepts quickly when engaged, particularly in geometry and spatial awareness tasks. Frequent fatigue and ADD mean he often zones out halfway through multi-step reasoning problems, leaving them incomplete. Target: Use a checklist to tick off each operational step of a word problem as he completes it, preventing him from losing his place.
English: Oscar is a master of the unwritten word, a storyteller who refuses to commit a single line to the page. His verbal contributions are often startlingly perceptive, revealing a dry humor and a keen understanding of narrative stakes that his written work never reflects. He is currently Working Towards the Standard because the act of writing seems to drain him of his vital spirits; he is a portrait of total writer’s block.
Creative Arts: Oscar is a master of the unrecorded thought who prefers to live in the quiet shadows of the back row rather than under the studio lights. He possesses a very sharp, dry sense of humor and can identify the subtext of a script or the mood of a painting in seconds, yet he treats the actual tools of the craft as if they were too heavy to lift. He often makes a brilliant, whispered observation to a neighbor but then retreats into a heavy silence, acting as a spectator to the creative process rather than a participant. Because he finds the sustained effort of physical execution so exhausting, he frequently leaves his sketchbook blank and his lines unread.
PE: Oscar treats the touchline like a critic's gallery rather than a field of play. He has a very sharp eye for the tactical flow of a match and will often whisper a dry, perfectly timed observation about a teammate's error to anyone nearby, yet he seems to view the actual requirement to run as a personal grievance. He possesses a natural, jagged coordination, but he finds the sustained physical effort of a full game so exhausting that he frequently retreats into a heavy silence on the sidelines, acting as a spectator to the effort of others.
Sciences: Oscar treats the lab bench like a theoretical boardroom. He has a high-velocity brain for complex chemical bonding and can calculate a density value in his head before I have even finished writing the formula on the board. Yet, he seems to have a profound aversion to the actual hardware; he watches a Bunsen burner with a detached suspicion, as if the equipment might bite. He is incredibly articulate during our hypothesis phase, but when the time comes for the manual assembly of a circuit or a titration, he effectively outsources the entire physical process to his lab partner while he remains motionless.
Humanities: Oscar operates like a detached political analyst. In History or RE, the air in the room often changes when he decides to weigh in; he has a knack for cutting through the fluff of a debate to reach the cynical core of a motive or a movement. He grasps the interconnected nature of Geography with an ease that is almost frustrating given his complete lack of written output. He occupies his desk with a heavy, unmoving presence, watching the lesson unfold as if he were reviewing it for a broadsheet newspaper rather than participating in it. There is a profound depth to his understanding of social justice and historical consequence, yet he maintains a strict embargo on the use of his pen, leaving his folder as a silent testament to his unrecorded expertise.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Anxiety / Dysregulation. Flight risk when overwhelmed.
Transition Portrait: Jack is a boy who wants to do well but has a very short fuse when he feels he has failed or been treated unfairly. When dysregulated, he can become verbally aggressive and will attempt to leave the classroom to avoid the situation. He responds best to a calm, low-arousal approach, clear boundaries, and the use of a 'time out' card to cool down in pastoral.
Home Life & Interests: Jack lives with his dad, and they have a very strong bond, often spending their weekends doing high-adrenaline activities together. Jack is an avid mountain biker and loves hitting the local trails, which acts as a great release for his pent-up energy. His dad is highly cooperative with the school's behavior team.
Maths: Strong mental math skills, particularly when applying math to real-world contexts like speed, distance, and time. Highly reactive. Requires heavy behavioral scaffolding to maintain focus and not disrupt others during written assessments. Target: Complete independent tasks in manageable 10-minute bursts before requesting a "check-in" with the teacher.
English: Loves dynamic, action-packed texts and brings fantastic enthusiasm to drama and reading aloud. Highly reactive; struggles to maintain the focus required for silent, sustained reading or extended independent writing blocks. Target: Break reading and writing tasks into highly focused 10-minute intervals, separated by brief, structured "brain breaks."
Creative Arts: Jack has so much energy! He loves playing the drums and doing physical comedy where he gets to fall over or run around. He cannot sit still for long, quiet drawing tasks, and he will end up poking his friends with a paintbrush if he gets bored. His target is to be given "active" roles in art, like being the one to hand out the paper or wash the pots, to give him an excuse to move around safely.
PE: Jack has absolutely boundless energy and loves hitting things hard, which makes him great at cricket and rounders! His hyperactivity makes him quite dangerous with equipment, though; he swings hockey sticks too high and throws balls at people's heads without thinking. He would improve if he completes a strict, slow-motion safety drill with the equipment before every lesson, learning how to control his raw power safely.
Sciences: Jack has so much energy and finds the practical side of science thrilling. He cannot sit still for long, quiet write-ups, and if he gets bored, he will start swinging on his lab stool or messing with the gas taps, which is a massive safety risk. His target is to earn his "lab license" every single lesson by demonstrating five minutes of calm, safe sitting before he is allowed to join the practical.
Humanities: Jack has boundless energy and absolutely loved it when we went outside to act out the formation of a volcano! He cannot cope with quiet, sustained reading, and if asked to read a two-page History source quietly, he will start kicking the desk or distracting his peers. He could do better if he breaks all reading tasks into tiny chunks, reading for just two minutes before being allowed to highlight a word or discuss it out loud.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Dyspraxia (DCD). Handwriting is painful and illegible.
Transition Portrait: Layla is a bright girl whose academic output is severely hampered by her dyspraxia. Her fine motor skills are very poor, making handwriting physically painful and almost entirely illegible. Providing her with a laptop for all written tasks is essential, and she also requires extra time and scaffolding in practical subjects like DT or Science to safely manipulate equipment.
Home Life & Interests: Layla lives with her dad, who is highly proactive in helping her manage her dyspraxia. Despite her coordination issues, she is incredibly passionate about cooking and baking. Her dad has bought her adapted, ergonomic kitchen tools, allowing her to safely practice complex recipes at home, which brings her a great deal of joy.
Maths: Fantastic conceptual understanding of fractions, ratio, and proportion (often intuitively linked to her baking hobby). Dyspraxia makes using rulers, protractors, and laying out formal written calculations physically difficult and messy. Target: Use math books with larger grid squares to support the physical alignment of column methods and reduce cognitive and physical load.
English: Excellent at writing sequential, instructional texts (likely linked to her baking). Has fantastic, highly descriptive verbal ideas. Dyspraxia makes the physical act of handwriting exhausting and messy; she often cuts her brilliant ideas short just to stop writing. Target: Allow the use of a laptop for extended writing assessments, or provide wide-lined paper to help physically anchor her handwriting.
Creative Arts: Layla comes up with brilliant, highly descriptive ideas for plays and loves the idea of making beautiful art. However, her dyspraxia makes holding small paintbrushes, using scissors, or playing fiddly instruments like the recorder really frustrating and messy. Her target is to always be provided with large-scale materials—like thick sponge brushes, big sugar paper, or large percussion drums—to completely remove those physical barriers.
PE: Layla is a brilliant team player and always encourages her peers! Because of her dyspraxia, PE is incredibly challenging; tracking a moving ball, coordinating a run-up to kick, or maintaining her balance during gymnastics frequently results in falls and frustration. She could do much better in Year 7 if she is always provided with oversized, brightly colored equipment (like a soft beach ball instead of a tennis ball) to make catching and tracking visually and physically easier.
Sciences: Layla understands the scientific concepts beautifully, but her dyspraxia makes standard lab work a nightmare. Trying to pour chemicals from tiny test tubes or use fiddly tweezers almost always ends in spills, breakages, and tears of frustration. Her target for Year 7 is to always be provided with wide-necked beakers, large plastic jugs, and chunky pipettes so she can measure and pour independently.
Humanities: Layla understands historical and geographical concepts beautifully, but her dyspraxia makes standard map work a nightmare. Trying to draw tiny borders or write small labels on a map almost always ends in ripped paper and tears of frustration. She could do better if she is always provided with enlarged A3 maps or allowed to use digital mapping software on an iPad to remove the physical barriers.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed ADHD (Hyperactive Type). Needs a 'fiddle toy' and permission to stand.
Transition Portrait: Theodore is a very bright, funny boy who physically cannot sit still for a 60-minute lesson. He constantly rocks on his chair, taps his pen, and blurts out answers impulsively. Giving him a discrete fiddle toy and a 'get out of jail free' card to stand at the back of the room for five minutes drastically reduces low-level disruption.
Home Life & Interests: Theodore lives with his mum, who is deeply understanding of his ADHD and ensures he has plenty of physical outlets. He plays the drum kit—loudly and enthusiastically—which is the only time his mum says he is completely, hyper-focused. He is a very lovable boy but requires high-energy parenting and teaching.
Maths: Lightning-fast recall of math facts. Thrives on high-energy, competitive arithmetic challenges. Hyperactivity means he struggles to sit still for extended reasoning tasks. Written layout is often highly erratic and unreadable. Target: Use a mini-whiteboard to draft and map out the steps of a reasoning problem before committing it formally to his exercise book.
English: Brings incredible, infectious energy to drama and script-reading; loves performing and reading aloud. Cannot sit still to proofread; his writing is often a massive stream-of-consciousness with little to no punctuation or paragraphing. Target: Read written work aloud to himself—wherever he naturally pauses to take a breath, he must insert a full stop or comma.
Creative Arts: Theodore is wonderfully bouncy and enthusiastic, but he hits the glockenspiels way too hard and treats every Drama lesson like an excuse to run around screaming! He struggles to control his impulses when he has props or instruments in front of him. His target is to practice "statue" exercises, learning how to stand completely still and totally silent for 30 seconds at a time.
PE: Theodore treats the sports hall like a giant bouncy castle! He has boundless enthusiasm and loves rugby, but he is completely incapable of modulating his physical power; he tackles far too hard, swings bats wildly, and frequently knocks people over by accident. He could do better if he is restricted to non-contact versions of sports (like tag rugby instead of contact) until he learns how to control his impulses and spatial awareness safely.
Sciences: Theodore treats the science lab like a playground! His hyperactivity means he struggles to control his impulses; he will squeeze pipettes too hard and squirt water everywhere, or try to balance heavy weights on his head. Because lab safety is critical, his target is to complete a "dry run" of the experiment with empty beakers to practice the physical movements safely before any chemicals are introduced.
Humanities: Theodore treats History like a drama lesson; he absolutely loves acting out battles or pretending to be a Tudor king! When it is time to sit down and write a summary of the lesson, he simply cannot sit still and his writing becomes an illegible, rushed mess. He could do better if he is allowed to verbally record his historical summaries on a tablet while standing up, rather than being forced to sit and write.

Transition Portrait: Dylan is an exceptionally mature and respectful young man who possesses a strong moral compass. He is thoughtful in his answers, treats his peers with kindness, and frequently stays behind to help teachers tidy the classroom. He is a natural candidate for student leadership roles, as other students genuinely respect him and listen to his advice.
Home Life & Interests: Dylan lives with his dad, who has instilled a strong sense of civic duty in him. Dylan volunteers his weekend mornings helping out at a local food bank and charity shop, which has given him a broad perspective on the world and a deep sense of empathy for those less fortunate.
Maths: Deeply mature and logical problem solver. Tackles complex reasoning tasks systematically and acts as an excellent peer-tutor. Can sometimes be slightly rigid in his preferred methods; needs encouragement to explore alternative, more efficient mathematical strategies. Target: Investigate and compare two different methods for solving the same reasoning problem and articulate which is more mathematically efficient.
English: Highly mature and articulate. Writes excellent, authoritative formal letters, speeches, and non-fiction reports. Can be slightly too rigid and formal in his creative writing; his narratives sometimes lack emotional warmth or sensory flair. Target: Experiment with varying sentence lengths (using highly controlled short, punchy sentences) to build tension and dramatic effect in fiction.
Creative Arts: Dylan is deeply mature and takes himself very, very seriously. He writes brilliant, serious scripts, but if you ask him to play a silly game like "musical statues" or act like a monkey, he will cross his arms and refuse. His target for Year 7 Drama is to throw himself into a completely ridiculous slapstick or clowning routine to learn that it is perfectly okay to look silly sometimes.
PE: Dylan takes himself very, very seriously. He is a strong runner and plays a very disciplined game of football, but if the teacher tries to play a silly, high-energy warm-up game like "Stuck in the Mud," he will cross his arms and refuse to join in, viewing it as childish. He could do better if he is occasionally challenged to let his guard down and participate in unstructured, silly games to remember that sport is also supposed to be fun.
Sciences: Dylan takes himself very seriously and expects science to be perfectly sterile, professional, and quiet. He gets incredibly annoyed by classmates who laugh at biology terms or want to make messy, colorful explosions just for fun. His target for Year 7 is to work with a partner to design a slightly silly or messy experiment, learning that science doesn't always have to be rigidly serious.
Humanities: Dylan is deeply mature and writes brilliant, highly serious essays on geopolitical history. He is so rigid, however, that his historical writing completely lacks any emotional warmth or empathy for the people involved. He could do better if he is occasionally challenged to write from the perspective of a child in history, forcing him to step away from his overly academic tone and access some empathy.

SEND Detail: Diagnosed Dyslexia. Processing speed in the 5th percentile. Struggles decoding unfamiliar words.
Transition Portrait: Leo has excellent verbal reasoning skills and can explain complex mathematical or scientific concepts aloud with ease. However, when faced with extended writing tasks, he quickly becomes avoidant and can act the clown to mask his struggles. Providing sentence starters and allowing him to type longer assignments drastically improves his output and confidence.
Home Life & Interests: Leo lives with his mum and dad, who are highly proactive regarding his dyslexia and regularly communicate with the school. Outside of the classroom, Leo is deeply invested in technology; he loves building custom PCs and coding simple games. He spends a significant amount of his free time gaming online with a close-knit group of friends.
Maths: Outstanding spatial reasoning. Excels in geometry, properties of 3D shapes, and coordinates. Dyslexia impacts his ability to decode heavy, text-based reasoning problems. Processing speed can be slow with wordy questions. Target: Use highlighting techniques to identify key mathematical operations hidden within dense word problems.
English: Leo leaves Year 6 as a classic literary protagonist trapped in a confusing script. His verbal dexterity is a marvel; in our discussions of complex narratives, he offers insights that are both sharp and startlingly mature. However, the physical act of writing remains a significant antagonist. His dyslexia transforms a simple paragraph into a labyrinth of phonetic traps, and his spelling acts like a thicket of thorns that obscures his brilliant logic. He is currently Working Towards the Standard in his written output, yet he is a high-achiever in his spirit and intellect. He requires a secondary environment that prioritizes his voice over his orthography, utilizing every digital tool available to ensure his stories are not buried under the weight of a stubborn pencil.
Creative Arts: Leo is brilliant at building things; he loves using clay or Lego in Art and has great 3D spatial skills. Because of his dyslexia, he finds reading scripts out loud in Drama really stressful and will often try to hide at the back. Moving into Year 7, his target is to do roleplay or improvised drama where he doesn't have to read anything on the spot, or to have his lines given to him a week in advance.
PE: Leo is fantastic at gymnastics and trampolining; his spatial awareness and physical control are brilliant to watch. Because of his dyslexia, he struggles to process complex, multi-step tactical instructions during fast-paced team sports like netball, often ending up running in the wrong direction. He would improve if teachers use visual demonstrations and physical walkthroughs of tactics, rather than relying on verbal explanations.
Sciences: Leo has a brilliant, practical mind; he is the best in the class at building electrical circuits and understanding how pulleys and levers work! Because of his dyslexia, writing out a long, detailed method or conclusion is exhausting and he will often just write one sentence. His target for Year 7 is to be allowed to draw a large, clearly labeled diagram of the experiment instead of having to write a lengthy descriptive paragraph.
Humanities: Leo has brilliant spatial awareness, which makes him the absolute star of the class when it comes to Geography! He can read a six-figure grid reference on an Ordnance Survey map faster than anyone else. Because of his dyslexia, writing the long "Evacuee Diary" in History was exhausting and upsetting for him. He could do better if he was allowed to demonstrate his historical knowledge through physical timelines or verbal presentations rather than extended essay writing.

Transition Portrait: Charlie is the kind of student every teacher loves to have: reliable, polite, and always willing to give a task his best effort. He doesn't always grasp new concepts immediately, but he is not afraid to ask for help and will rework a problem until he understands it. He is a fantastic role model for resilience.
Home Life & Interests: Charlie lives with his dad, who runs a local independent convenience store. Charlie often helps out in the shop on weekends, stocking shelves and talking to customers, which has made him very socially mature and confident speaking to adults. He is grounded, understands the value of hard work, and saves his pocket money diligently.
Maths: A highly dependable, grounded mathematician. Exceptionally secure in his formal written methods for all four operations. Tends to play it safe. Will often choose the easier worksheet rather than risk making a mistake on the Greater Depth tasks. Target: Attempt the hardest extension or "challenge" question in every single lesson, even if unsure of the final answer.
English: Writes very clear, grammatically correct, and highly dependable non-fiction texts. Very cautious in creative writing; sticks to highly predictable, "safe" plotlines and simple vocabulary. Target: Experiment with non-linear narrative structures (e.g., starting a story in media res or using flashbacks).
Creative Arts: Charlie is a very sensible boy who always remembers to bring a pencil and follows the rules perfectly. In drama, though, he always plays it completely safe, usually volunteering to be a tree, a wall, or the narrator just so he doesn't have to act. To push him out of his comfort zone, his Year 7 target is to take on a speaking character with at least three lines in every group play.
PE: Charlie is a very sensible boy who always remembers his kit and tries his absolute best at every single sport we do, even though he is not naturally coordinated. He runs in a slightly awkward way and often trips over his own feet in football. He would improve if he is given specific, targeted exercises to develop his core strength, agility, and fundamental movement skills rather than just being thrown into competitive matches.
Sciences: Charlie is the safest boy in the classroom; he will happily wear his safety goggles for the entire hour and follow the instructions to the letter. However, he refuses to guess what might happen in an experiment just in case his hypothesis is incorrect. To push his scientific thinking, his target is to make a completely wild, "wrong" prediction on purpose to learn that a hypothesis is just an educated guess.
Humanities: Charlie is a very sensible boy who likes knowing exactly what he needs to do to get a tick. In RE, when asked for his opinion on a moral issue, he will just copy whatever the textbook says to ensure he gets it "right." He could do better by writing down one completely original opinion or thought about a religious festival or moral issue, without being allowed to look at a book.

Transition Portrait: Mason is an incredibly curious and highly motivated learner who is never satisfied with surface-level explanations. He asks a barrage of "why" and "how" questions during lessons, which is fantastic for engagement but can sometimes derail the teacher's lesson plan. He loves project-based learning where he can research a topic deeply and present his findings.
Home Life & Interests: Mason lives with his dad, and they share a deep love of history and science. Mason's evenings are often spent watching lengthy historical documentaries or reading non-fiction books rather than playing video games. He has an adult-like conversational style and enjoys debating current events with his dad over dinner.
Maths: A highly analytical thinker who excels at finding complex patterns in number sequences. Loves the history of math. Can get bogged down in the "why" of a mathematical rule and lose track of actually completing the calculation practice. Target: Practice translating his excellent verbal observations of number patterns into formal algebraic notation (e.g., finding the nth term).
English: Excellent at cross-referencing historical context with literature; very strong non-fiction reading and retrieval skills. Creative writing often reads like a history textbook, lacking emotional depth, sensory details, or character development. Target: Focus heavily on "show, don't tell" techniques to convey a character's emotions rather than simply listing their actions.
Creative Arts: Mason loves history, so if the class play is about the Romans or Tudors, he is fully on board and will memorize all the facts! He struggles with emotional acting, though, and gets really annoyed if someone in his group wants to do something silly or historically inaccurate. His target is to invent a completely ridiculous, fictional character for a play, just to practice being creative rather than factual.
PE: Mason has a brilliant memory and knows the absolute, strict rules of every sport we play, especially cricket! He acts more like an umpire than a player, though, frequently stopping the game to loudly point out when a classmate has foot-faulted or traveled with the ball, which causes arguments. He would improve if he is explicitly told he is not allowed to referee his peers, forcing him to focus entirely on his own physical performance.
Sciences: Mason loves the history of science and can tell you all about Isaac Newton or Charles Darwin! However, he is very rigid; if his classroom experiment doesn't give the exact result that the textbook says it should, he gets very argumentative and says the equipment is broken. His target is to practice writing evaluations that explain why real-world classroom experiments aren't as perfect as professional labs.
Humanities: Mason is a walking encyclopedia of historical facts and absolutely loves learning about the Romans and the Victorians! However, he finds empathetic writing (like writing a diary entry as a poor Victorian child) completely pointless and will often just write a list of facts instead. He would improve if he is allowed to create factual "character profiles" or newspaper reports, playing to his strengths rather than forcing him to write emotionally.

SEND Detail: Suspected Selective Mutism. Freezes when called upon directly in class. Nodding only.
Transition Portrait: Scarlett's anxiety in the classroom is so profound that she borders on selective mutism. She will not speak to teachers under any circumstances, communicating only through nods, shakes of the head, or writing on a whiteboard. She produces neat work but will never ask for help when stuck, meaning teachers must closely monitor her progress over her shoulder.
Home Life & Interests: Scarlett lives with her dad, who describes her as a "chatterbox" at home, highlighting that her anxiety is purely situational to the school environment. She finds comfort in animals and spends all her free time caring for her two pet rabbits. She dreams of working in an animal sanctuary where she doesn't have to talk to people.
Maths: Selective mutism means all assessments are written. Her math book reveals a highly accurate, careful mathematician secure in KS2. Cannot be assessed verbally. Requires gentle, non-verbal check-ins to ensure she understands new, abstract concepts. Target: Utilize a red/amber/green card system on her desk so she can signal for help without needing to speak.
English: Produces beautiful, highly sensitive written analysis of characters and poetry. Immaculate presentation. Selective Mutism means she cannot participate in verbal debates, group drama, or read aloud under any circumstances. Target: Allow her to demonstrate her "speaking and listening" competencies entirely through written dialogue and scripted roleplay.
Creative Arts: Scarlett's selective mutism means she cannot speak in Drama or Music, but her artwork is absolutely breathtaking, and she expresses all her feelings on the page. To include her in Drama without causing her distress, her target is to be the group's "puppeteer" or to do entirely mime-based performances where she doesn't have to make a single sound.
PE: Scarlett is a fantastic athlete, particularly in individual sports like long jump or sprinting, where her selective mutism is not a barrier. In team sports, her inability to shout for a pass or communicate a strategy makes invasion games very difficult for her to navigate. She could do better in Year 7 if she and her team are explicitly taught to use non-verbal hand signals and eye contact to communicate effectively on the pitch.
Sciences: Scarlett's selective mutism means she cannot participate in the noisy, verbal debates that often happen during group planning. She is, however, brilliant at drawing highly accurate, labeled diagrams. Her target to ensure she is fully included in group work is to be given the official role of "Diagrammatic Recorder," taking charge of drawing the equipment setup.
Humanities: Scarlett's selective mutism means she cannot participate in verbal debates or read historical sources aloud. However, her written analysis of religious ethics is beautifully complex and thoughtful. She could do better in Year 7 if she is given a mini-whiteboard during class debates, allowing her to hold up her thoughts or vote on moral issues without having to make a sound.

Transition Portrait: David is a good-natured, well-meaning boy who simply loves to talk. His low-level disruption is never malicious, but he struggles to filter his thoughts and will chatter away to whoever is sitting next to him. He works best when seated near the teacher's desk and away from his main friendship group, allowing him to focus on his independent work.
Home Life & Interests: David lives with his dad, and they have a highly active, sports-focused relationship. David plays youth rugby and is a dedicated team player who handles the physical nature of the sport well. Because he expends so much physical energy on the weekends, he can sometimes arrive on Monday morning feeling quite lethargic.
Maths: Highly enthusiastic learner who happily volunteers answers. Has a solid baseline grasp of fractions and decimals. Frequently guesses answers based on the first number he sees, completely failing to read the actual question being asked. Target: Read every reasoning question twice and underline the final command word (e.g., "calculate the difference") before starting.
English: Confident, chatty, and happy to read aloud. Brings a great, good-natured energy to collaborative group tasks. Often guesses words based on the first letter rather than sounding them out, leading to completely incorrect reading comprehension. Target: Track the text physically with a ruler or finger while reading aloud to force him to look at every single word and punctuation mark.
Creative Arts: David is a lovely, cheerful boy who will confidently belt out the completely wrong words to a song at the top of his lungs! In Art, he just plunges his brush into whatever paint is closest without checking the color, resulting in a lot of muddy brown paper. His target is to look closely at the lyrics sheet or the paint palette for three seconds before he jumps into action.
PE: David is incredibly cheerful and has fantastic physical power; he can hit a cricket ball or a tennis ball an absolute mile! He has zero patience for precision, however, meaning he usually hits the ball completely out of bounds or straight into the net because he isn't looking where he is aiming. He could do better if he practices targeting drills, where he only scores points for hitting the ball gently into a specific, marked zone rather than just hitting it hard.
Sciences: David is wonderfully cheerful, but he has zero patience for precision! If the instructions say "add the powder slowly, stirring constantly," he will just dump the entire pot of powder in at once and vigorously shake the beaker. His target is to practice using a dropping pipette to add liquids one single drop at a time, forcing him to slow his physical movements down.
Humanities: David is wonderfully cheerful but has zero patience for precision! If asked to locate a country in an atlas, he will make a wild guess, point to a random shape, and confidently declare he has found it. He could do better if he is partnered with a more methodical student and explicitly required to cross-reference his guesses with the index of an atlas before writing anything down.

Transition Portrait: Ivy is an exceptionally studious and polite girl who sets incredibly high expectations for herself in every subject. She absorbs information like a sponge and presents her work beautifully, though she can be overly self-critical if she scores less than full marks. She is a quiet presence in the room but a highly valued member of any academic team.
Home Life & Interests: Ivy lives in a busy, multi-generational household where educational achievement is highly prized and actively supported. She is deeply respectful of her elders and takes her studies very seriously. In her downtime, she is a prolific reader, often retreating to her room with a stack of library books ranging from history to classic literature.
Maths: Works with meticulous precision, particularly in geometry and statistics. Consistently achieves near-perfect marks in arithmetic. Highly rigid. If her preferred method fails to yield an answer, she struggles to pivot to an alternative strategy. Target: Develop and apply a secondary checking strategy (such as working backward or using an inverse operation) when stuck.
English: Immaculate handwriting and flawless grammar. Excels at highly structured writing tasks like formal letters or reports. Very rigid thinker; struggles with open-ended creative tasks where there isn't a strict "right" answer or formula to follow. Target: Participate in collaborative, round-robin story-writing exercises where peers introduce unexpected plot twists.
Creative Arts: Ivy wants to know exactly what the rules are. Her artwork is incredibly precise, and she loves using a ruler to make sure her borders are perfect. She gets really stressed in drama if we do improvisation because there isn't a script and she doesn't know what's going to happen next. Her target is to play quick, silly improv games where the rules constantly change, helping her learn to think on her feet without panicking.
PE: Ivy loves athletics and fitness tests because she wants to know exactly what her score is so she can beat it. She gets very stressed in team games because she is a stickler for the rules; if the teacher misses a foul or a ball going out of bounds, Ivy will stop playing entirely to argue about it. She would improve if she practices accepting the referee's final decision without arguing, learning that human error is just a part of playing sports.
Sciences: Ivy wants science to be an exact set of rules. She is the student who will spend ten minutes trying to measure exactly 10.0ml of water in a measuring cylinder, panicking if it hits 10.1ml. She gets very stressed if an experiment doesn't work perfectly the first time. Her target is to learn about "margins of error" in science, practicing accepting measurements that are simply "close enough."
Humanities: Ivy loves Geography because maps have strict borders, keys, and rules. She gets very stressed in History because she wants one simple answer to questions like "Why did the Victorians use workhouses?", and gets upset when the teacher explains there are multiple, complex reasons. She could do better by practicing phrase starters like "On the one hand... but on the other hand..." to help her accept that history is rarely black and white.

Transition Portrait: Jayden is a lively and boisterous character who brings a lot of energy to the classroom, though this can sometimes spill over into disruption. He responds exceptionally well to positive reinforcement and being given small positions of responsibility, like handing out books. If reprimanded publicly, he is prone to sulking, so quiet, 1-to-1 corrections work best.
Home Life & Interests: Jayden lives with his maternal grandmother, who is deeply invested in his success but sometimes struggles to manage his boundless energy at home. He plays for a local youth football league, which provides an excellent outlet for him. His grandmother notes that he eats a very limited diet and can be fussy, which occasionally affects his energy levels.
Maths: Energetic participation in class. Rapid and secure recall of multiplication tables up to 12x12. Written layout is often messy, causing place-value errors in long multiplication and division calculations. Target: Use grid paper to strictly align place value columns during formal written calculations.
English: Enthusiastic reader of non-fiction. Brings fantastic energy and volume to drama and speaking/listening tasks. Struggles with the stamina required for extended writing; handwriting and spelling degrade significantly after 10 minutes. Target: Build writing stamina through short, highly focused 10-minute "free writing" bursts to improve physical endurance.
Creative Arts: Jayden is the class clown and loves making everyone laugh during physical drama games. The problem is he doesn't know when to stop, and he will often pull silly faces or make noises when it's someone else's turn to perform. His target for Year 7 is to practice being a good audience member, sitting completely still and quiet while his classmates are doing their plays.
PE: Jayden has endless stamina and loves the physical chaos of invasion games! The problem is that he treats every PE lesson like a joke, deliberately breaking the rules in rounders or doing silly dances on the pitch to make the rest of the class laugh, completely ruining the game. He could do better if he is given the responsibility of refereeing a short game, forcing him to understand and enforce the rules he usually ignores.
Sciences: Jayden thinks science is just magic tricks and explosions! He is so excited by the practicals that he will often mess around with his safety goggles, poke his partner with a plastic pipette, or shout out. Because lab safety is so important in Year 7, his target is to successfully demonstrate calm, sensible behavior for the first five minutes of a lesson before he earns his "license" to participate in the practical.
Humanities: Jayden finds it impossible to take RE seriously and will frequently try to make his friends laugh during quiet moments of reflection or when discussing serious ethical topics. He is easily engaged by the "gross" bits of history, like mummification, but lacks the stamina to write about it. He could do better if he practiced sustaining just three minutes of sensible, quiet reflection or reading at the start of a Humanities lesson before he is allowed to discuss his ideas.

Transition Portrait: Phoebe is an exceptional English student with a vocabulary that rivals many adults. She writes with incredible flair, persuasion, and emotional depth, frequently reading far above her chronological age. In subjects she cares less about, like PE or DT, she can be somewhat dismissive and needs reminding that all subjects hold value.
Home Life & Interests: Phoebe's home is filled with literature, and her parents actively encourage her to debate politics and current affairs at the dinner table. She has her sights set firmly on becoming an investigative journalist and has already started writing articles for the primary school newsletter. She is highly driven and fiercely independent.
Maths: Working at Greater Depth. Interrogates data and statistics with a critical eye, constructing beautifully logical arguments. Can be highly critical of peers who do not grasp concepts as quickly as she does, leading to friction during collaborative tasks. Target: Channel her intellectual confidence into leading group investigations, focusing on encouraging peers rather than directing them.
English: Exceptional non-fiction writer; her persuasive articles are highly sophisticated, well-researched, and commanding. Can be overly critical, blunt, and dismissive of peers' ideas during group discussions or peer-assessment tasks. Target: Frame all peer feedback using a strict "two stars and a wish" model to ensure her critiques remain constructive and polite.
Creative Arts: Phoebe is very clever and quick to pick up musical notes, but she can be incredibly bossy. She will happily tell another 11-year-old that they are singing out of tune or that they are holding their paintbrush wrong, which causes a lot of arguments. Her target is to give two genuine compliments to her classmates before she is allowed to offer any "improvements" on their work.
PE: Phoebe is incredibly fit, highly competitive, and plays a fantastic game of netball! She is terribly bossy, though, and will spend the entire match shouting at her teammates, telling them exactly where to stand and getting angry if they don't pass to her. She would improve if she is given a "silent match" challenge, where she is banned from speaking and must rely entirely on non-verbal communication and trusting her teammates' decisions.
Sciences: Phoebe is incredibly smart and understands the scientific concepts perfectly, but she is terribly bossy! She will literally snatch a thermometer or a beaker out of her lab partner's hands if she thinks they are measuring something too slowly or inaccurately. Her target is to practice taking turns—she can read the method aloud, but she must keep her hands behind her back while her partner pours the liquids.
Humanities: Phoebe is incredibly smart and understands the geopolitical reasons behind historical events perfectly. She is terribly bossy during group Geography projects, however, often snatching the map out of her friends' hands to draw it herself because they are "too slow." She would improve if she is given the strict role of "Lead Researcher," meaning she has to find the facts in the atlas while someone else does the drawing.

Transition Portrait: Edward is a solid, reliable student who particularly enjoys subjects that deal with facts, dates, and the physical world, like history and geography. He is very literal and can struggle with empathy tasks (e.g., "Imagine how this character felt"), preferring to analyze the mechanics of an event rather than the emotion. He is always polite and well-equipped.
Home Life & Interests: Edward lives with his parents and is an incredibly keen amateur historian. His favorite hobby is metal detecting; he spends hours walking fields with his dad hoping to find Roman coins or Victorian relics. He frequently brings his finds into school to show his history teachers and is incredibly proud of his collection.
Maths: Excels in concrete arithmetic and measurement. Interprets mathematical vocabulary highly literally. Struggles significantly when reasoning word problems contain unnecessary narrative "fluff" or abstract idioms. Target: Practice systematically crossing out the non-mathematical, narrative text in word problems to isolate the core calculation.
English: Exceptional non-fiction reader. Seamlessly incorporates his vast historical knowledge into his English work. Very literal interpretation of texts. Struggles to understand why an author would use a simile instead of just stating a fact. Target: Practice writing "show, don't tell" paragraphs, focusing heavily on conveying emotion through physical description.
Creative Arts: Edward is obsessed with facts. If his group is doing a play set in Victorian times, he will stop the whole scene to argue that someone's imaginary hat isn't historically accurate! He prefers drawing diagrams of machines over expressive art. His target is to let go of the facts for one lesson and act out a completely made-up fantasy or sci-fi scene.
PE: Edward is obsessed with the history and statistics of sports! He would much rather stand on the sidelines and talk to the teacher about the history of the Olympics or the rules of 19th-century cricket than actually pick up a bat and play. He would improve if his vast knowledge is utilized by making him the official statistician for the class, but only on the condition that he plays the first half of the match before picking up his clipboard.
Sciences: Edward loves facts and history. During an experiment, he will happily spend twenty minutes arguing with his partner about the history of the scientist who invented the microscope, completely ignoring the actual microscope sitting in front of him! His target is to focus purely on the present-day practical, recording three real-time observations before he is allowed to share historical facts.
Humanities: Edward is obsessed with factual accuracy. If his group is writing a play about the Victorians, he will stop the entire lesson to argue that a classmate's imaginary hat isn't historically accurate for the year 1852! He would improve if he is challenged to explore why historical myths and legends exist, learning to value the storytelling aspect of history alongside the hard facts.

Transition Portrait: Evelyn is a very hardworking student who takes her education seriously and always completes her homework to a good standard. Unfortunately, she suffers from severe performance anxiety; during formal assessments, she panics, her mind goes blank, and she underperforms compared to her classwork. She needs specific coaching on exam techniques and anxiety management.
Home Life & Interests: Evelyn lives with her mum and stepdad in a very supportive household. She has been dancing since she was a toddler and currently studies ballet at a high grade, which requires immense physical and mental discipline. She uses dance as a way to relieve stress, but the pressure of dance exams occasionally exacerbates her general anxiety.
Maths: Highly accurate in arithmetic operations. Approaches her math book with rigid, flawless perfectionism. Becomes visibly stressed, sometimes to the point of tears, when a reasoning problem does not have an immediate, obvious solution. Target: Build resilience by explicitly practicing "trial and improvement" methods, learning that getting it wrong is a valid step.
English: Produces highly structured, deeply analytical paragraphs using the PEE/PEEL framework perfectly. Rigid perfectionism leads to meltdowns if she makes a spelling mistake; will frequently tear pages out of her book to start again. Target: Use a mini-whiteboard for the initial drafting phase so mistakes can be easily wiped away without the permanence of pen on paper.
Creative Arts: Evelyn will practice a keyboard tune over and over until it is flawless. Her extreme perfectionism means that if she hits one wrong note, or accidentally smudges her painting, she will burst into tears and want to throw the whole thing in the bin. To help her build resilience, her target is to play a "wrong" note on purpose during a warm-up game to learn that the world doesn't end when mistakes happen.
PE: Evelyn is a beautiful gymnast and takes incredible pride in pointing her toes and perfecting her routines. Her extreme perfectionism means she cannot cope with losing; if her team loses a casual game of rounders, she will burst into tears and declare the game was unfair. She could do better if she is explicitly praised for her effort and sportsmanship in losing scenarios, learning that you can play a great game and still not win.
Sciences: Evelyn's extreme perfectionism makes recording data very stressful. If she writes down a number in her results table and then realizes she measured it wrong, she will try to rub it out so fiercely that she tears the page, rather than just crossing it out. Her target for Year 7 is to record all her raw data in pen, crossing out anomalous results with a single neat line to learn that mistakes are just part of science.
Humanities: Evelyn creates the most flawless, beautifully shaded Geography maps in the class. Unfortunately, her extreme perfectionism means that if she accidentally colors outside the line on a map of Europe, she will burst into tears and want to throw the entire worksheet away. She could do better by completing all her Geography map work using erasable colored pencils, completely removing the permanence of a mistake.

SEND Detail: Suspected EBSA. High anxiety around peers, poor attendance.
Transition Portrait: When Evie is in the classroom, she is a bright, perceptive, and capable student who produces excellent written work. However, her attendance dropped to 78% in Year 6 due to severe, anxiety-driven school avoidance. She needs a very gentle, nurturing approach and a designated staff member she can check in with every morning to feel safe.
Home Life & Interests: Evie lives with her nan, who is extremely loving but struggles to enforce boundaries when Evie refuses to go to school. Evie's anxiety is primarily social; she worries intensely about what other girls think of her. When she feels safe at home, she is highly creative and loves acting out plays and learning dramatic monologues.
Maths: Has a very solid foundational understanding of place value, ordering decimals, and adding/subtracting fractions. High anxiety causes her to second-guess correct methods. Relies heavily on constant teacher validation to proceed. Target: Utilize a "three before me" strategy (check notes, check a resource, try once independently) before asking for teacher reassurance.
English: Excellent spelling and punctuation; produces neat, careful written work when she is emotionally regulated. Severe anxiety (EBSA profile) means she frequently misses lessons, creating massive gaps in her knowledge of class texts. Target: Use pre-recorded audiobooks and chapter summary guides at home to keep up with the class reader during absences.
Creative Arts: Evie does lovely, neat coloring and tries really hard when she is in the classroom. Sadly, her severe anxiety means she misses a lot of school, which makes group work in drama almost impossible because her group never knows if she will be there for the final performance. Her target is to be given solo art or digital music projects she can do on a tablet at home so she doesn't fall behind her classmates.
PE: Evie is a surprisingly fast sprinter and enjoys the short, sharp bursts of energy required for relay races. Sadly, her severe EBSA anxiety means she misses a lot of school, and returning to a loud, chaotic PE lesson is often too overwhelming for her, causing her to retreat to the pastoral office. She could do better if she is allowed to join a quiet, small-group intervention for PE (like yoga or walking) when she first transitions back into school.
Sciences: Evie produces beautiful, neat diagrams of plant cells and the water cycle when she is in the classroom. Sadly, her severe anxiety means she misses a lot of school, which means she frequently misses the hands-on practicals and struggles to understand how the experiments work. Her target is to watch video demonstrations of the practicals she misses at home so she doesn't fall behind her peers.
Humanities: Evie has a wonderful grasp of chronological history and her map coloring is phenomenally neat when she is regulated and in class. Sadly, her severe EBSA anxiety means she misses weeks of school at a time, completely losing the thread of our term-long History topics like the Ancient Greeks. She could do better if she uses interactive history games and digital map-reading tools at home to ensure she stays connected to the Humanities curriculum.
